title.zh: 卷六十三 李杜列傳
title.raw: 卷六十三 李杜列傳 Volume 63: Biographies of Li, Du

sentences.zh: 李固字子堅，漢中南鄭人，司徒合之子也。

translations.literal: Li Gu, courtesy name Zijian, was a man of Nan Zheng in Hanzhong, and was the son of Minister of Education Li He.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu, whose courtesy name was Zijian, came from Nan Zheng in Hanzhong; he was the son of Li He, who had held the office of Minister of Education.

sentences.zh: 合在*

translations.literal: He appears in the [text is damaged here; the commentary cross-reference is incomplete].
translations.idiomatic: The passage on Li He is damaged in this edition; the commentary’s cross-reference is incomplete.

translations.literal: (Several [instances / items; editorial mark in the source]).
translations.idiomatic: Editorial note in the received text: “several” (the exact enumeration is not preserved here).

translations.literal: See the biography of Fangshu [the graph is damaged; the commentary points to the treatise on techniques and arts].
translations.idiomatic: See the treatise on techniques and arts (the manuscript note is partly illegible).

sentences.zh: 固貌狀有奇表，鼎角匿犀，足履龜文。

translations.literal: Gu’s appearance had a remarkable aspect: tripod-horn brow, hidden rhinoceros [ridge], and tortoise-pattern tread on the soles of his feet.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu’s bearing was striking: his forehead rose in the “tripod-horn” shape, a “hidden rhinoceros” ridge ran up into his hairline, and the soles of his feet showed the tortoise-shell markings that physiognomy books associate with the rank of two thousand piculs.

sentences.zh: [一]少好學，常步行尋師，不遠千里。

translations.literal: [Note 1] In youth he loved learning; he often went on foot to seek teachers and did not regard a thousand li as too far.
translations.idiomatic: From boyhood he loved scholarship; he would walk long distances to study under a master and thought nothing of a journey of a thousand li.

sentences.zh: [二]遂究覽墳籍，結交英賢。

translations.literal: [Note 2] Thereupon he exhaustively perused the classics and records and formed friendships with outstanding and worthy men.
translations.idiomatic: He went on to master the classical corpus and win the friendship of the ablest men of his day.

sentences.zh: 四方有志之士，多慕其風而來學。

translations.literal: Men of purpose from the four quarters, many admiring his manner, came to study with him.
translations.idiomatic: Scholars from every region who aspired to something higher admired his example and flocked to learn from him.

sentences.zh: 京師鹹歎曰：「是復為李公矣。

translations.literal: In the capital all sighed and said, “This is again a Lord Li.”
translations.idiomatic: In the capital people murmured with approval: “Here is another Li who will rise to the highest office.”

sentences.zh: 」[三]司隸﹑益州並命郡舉孝廉，辟司空掾，皆不就。

translations.literal: ” [Note 3] The Metropolitan Governor and the Inspector of Yi Province together ordered the commandery to recommend him as filial and incorrupt, and he was summoned as an aide to the Minister of Works; in each case he did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: ” The Metropolitan Governor and the Inspector of Yi Province both instructed his commandery to nominate him as “filial and incorrupt,” and the Minister of Works tried to appoint him as an aide; he declined every offer.

translations.literal: [Note 4]
translations.idiomatic: Note 4.

sentences.zh: 注[一]鼎角者，頂有骨如鼎足也。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: “Tripod-horn” means there is bone on the crown of the head like the legs of a tripod.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 1: the “tripod-horn” brow means a bony prominence on the vertex shaped like a tripod’s legs.

sentences.zh: 匿犀，伏犀也。

translations.literal: “Hidden rhinoceros” is the “crouching rhinoceros.”
translations.idiomatic: “Hidden rhinoceros” is the same as the physiognomists’ “crouching rhinoceros” forehead.

sentences.zh: 謂骨當額上入髮際隱起也。

translations.literal: It means the bone on the forehead, entering the hairline, rises hidden.
translations.idiomatic: That is, a ridge along the forehead that runs up into the hairline and shows only as a subtle rise.

sentences.zh: 足履龜文者二千石，見相書。

translations.literal: One whose foot tread has tortoise pattern is [fit for rank of] two thousand piculs; see the physiognomy books.
translations.idiomatic: Tortoise-shell markings on the soles mark a man for the salary grade of two thousand piculs, according to the manuals of physiognomy.

sentences.zh: 注[二]謝承書曰：「固改易姓名，杖策驅驢，負笈追師三輔，學五經，積十餘年。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Xie Cheng’s book states: “Gu changed his name, staff in hand drove a donkey, carried a book-box and pursued teachers in the Three Adjuncts, studied the Five Classics, and accumulated more than ten years.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 2: Xie Cheng’s history says Li Gu traveled incognito, staff and donkey his only equipage, book-box on his back, seeking instruction in the capital region for well over a decade as he mastered the Five Classics.

sentences.zh: 博覽古今，明於風角﹑星筭﹑河圖﹑讖緯，仰察俯占，窮神知變。

translations.literal: Broadly perusing ancient and modern, he was clear concerning wind-angles, star reckoning, River Chart, and prognosticatory weft-texts; looking up he observed, looking down he divined, exhausting spirit and knowing transformation.
translations.idiomatic: He read widely across past and present and became expert in wind-angles, astrological calculation, the River Chart, and apocryphal weft-texts; he scanned heaven above and divined by earthly signs until he had plumbed the subtlest shifts of fate.

sentences.zh: 每到太學，密入公府，定省父母，不令同業諸生知是合子。」

translations.literal: Whenever he arrived at the Imperial Academy, he secretly entered the high minister’s residence, attended upon and inquired after his parents, and did not allow the fellow students of the same pursuit to know he was He’s son.”
translations.idiomatic: Whenever he came to the Imperial Academy he would slip into his father’s official compound to visit his parents, and he kept his fellow students from learning that he was Li He’s son.”

sentences.zh: 注[三]言復繼其父為公也。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: It says he would again succeed his father as duke [i.e., as one of the Three Excellencies].
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 3: the remark means people expected him to follow his father to one of the highest ministerial posts.

sentences.zh: 注[四]謝承書曰：「五察孝廉，益州再舉茂才，不應。

translations.literal: Comment [4] Xie Cheng’s book states: “Five times he was investigated as filial and incorrupt; Yi Province twice recommended him as flourishing talent; he did not respond.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 4: Xie Cheng records that the commandery nominated him five times as “filial and incorrupt,” that Yi Province twice put him forward as “flourishing talent,” and that he refused each summons.

sentences.zh: 五府連辟，皆辭以疾。」

translations.literal: The Five Offices repeatedly summoned him; in each case he declined citing illness.”
translations.idiomatic: The chief bureaus of state called him in turn, and each time he pleaded illness and stayed away.”

sentences.zh: 陽嘉二年，有地動﹑山崩﹑火□之異，公卿舉固對策，[一]詔又特問當世之敝，為政所宜。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yangjia there were anomalies of earthquake, mountain collapse, and fire [text damaged]; the excellencies and ministers recommended Gu to reply to the policy questions, [note 1] and an edict also specially asked about the defects of the present age and what was fitting in governing.
translations.idiomatic: In the second year of the Yangjia era (133 CE), earthquakes, landslides, and strange fires [one character illegible in the text] alarmed the court; the high ministers nominated Li Gu to answer the imperial policy questionnaire, and a follow-up edict pressed him on the ills of the day and what good government required.

sentences.zh: 固對曰： 注[一]續漢書曰「陽嘉二年，詔公卿舉敦樸之士，□尉賈建舉固」也。

translations.literal: Gu replied, saying: Comment [1] the Continuation of the Han Documents states: “In the second year of Yangjia, an edict ordered the excellencies and ministers to recommend honest and plain scholars; [damaged] Commandant Jia Jian recommended Gu.”
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu submitted his memorial. Commentary, note 1: the Continuation of the Han Documents records that in Yangjia 2 the throne told the Three Excellencies and nine ministers to nominate men of plain integrity; Commandant Jia Jian [one title character damaged] put forward Li Gu.

sentences.zh: 注[一]春秋感精符曰：「人主日月同明，四時合信，故父天母地，兄日姊月。

translations.literal: Comment [1] the Spring and Autumn Sensitivity to Essence and Tokens states: “The lord of men shares brightness with sun and moon; the four seasons combine in trustworthiness; therefore he fathers Heaven and mothers Earth, elder-brothers the Sun and elder-sisters the Moon.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 1: the apocryphon Spring and Autumn Sensitivity to Essence and Tokens says the ruler shares the radiance of sun and moon, moves in step with the four seasons, and therefore stands as “father to Heaven and mother to Earth,” with the sun as elder brother and the moon as elder sister.

sentences.zh: 」宋均注曰：「父天於圜丘之祀也，母地於方澤之祭也，兄日於東郊，姊月於西郊。」

translations.literal: ” Song Jun’s commentary states: “Fathering Heaven is in the suburban sacrifice at the round mound; mothering Earth is in the sacrifice at the square marsh; elder-brothering the Sun is at the eastern suburb; elder-sistering the Moon is at the western suburb.”
translations.idiomatic: ” Song Jun glosses this as ritual language: the emperor “fathers Heaven” at the round-mound sacrifice, “mothers Earth” at the square-marsh rite, honors the sun at the eastern suburb, and the moon at the western suburb.

sentences.zh: 注[二]史記曰：「魏武侯浮西河而下，中河顧而謂吳起曰：『美哉乎河山之固，此魏之寶也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] the Records of the Grand Historian states: “Marquis Wu of Wei floated down the western River; mid-river he looked back and said to Wu Qi: ‘How beautiful is the firmness of river and mountains—this is Wei’s treasure.’”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 2: Sima Qian tells how Marquis Wu of Wei, drifting down the western bend of the Yellow River, turned to Wu Qi and exclaimed that the strength of river and hill was Wei’s true wealth.

sentences.zh: 』吳起對曰：『在德不在險。

translations.literal: ’ Wu Qi replied, saying: ‘It lies in virtue, not in terrain.’
translations.idiomatic: ’ Wu Qi answered, ‘Security rests on virtue, not on natural barriers.’

sentences.zh: 』」注[三]命，爵命也。

translations.literal: ’” Comment [3]: “Mandate” means enfeoffment mandate.
translations.idiomatic: ’” Commentary, note 3: “mandate” here means the grant of rank and title.

sentences.zh: 言有德者乃可加爵命也。

translations.literal: It means one who has virtue may then be given enfeoffment mandate.
translations.idiomatic: The point is that only a man of real moral weight should receive such honors.

sentences.zh: 注[四]阿母王聖。

translations.literal: Comment [4]: the nurse-nanny Wang Sheng.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 4: the wet-nurse Wang Sheng.

sentences.zh: 注[五]謂順帝為太子時，廢為濟陰王。

translations.literal: Comment [5]: it refers to when Shun-di was heir apparent and was deposed and made Prince of Jiyin.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 5: the allusion is to Emperor Shun’s deposition, while still heir apparent, to the minor title of Prince of Jiyin.

sentences.zh: 注[六]殆，危也。

translations.literal: Comment [6]: dai means danger.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 6: the word dai means “peril” or “standing on the edge of ruin.”

sentences.zh: 注[七]沛然，寬廣之意。

translations.literal: Comment [7]: peiran is the idea of breadth and amplitude.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 7: peiran conveys breadth and liberality of spirit.

sentences.zh: 注[八]謂宋娥也。

translations.literal: Comment [8]: it refers to Song E.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 8: the woman meant is Song E.

sentences.zh: 夫妃後之家所以少完全者，豈天性當然？

translations.literal: As for the reason consort and empress houses seldom remain intact, is it that their nature is thus?
translations.idiomatic: Why is it that families tied to the harem so rarely end well? Surely that cannot be their inborn character.

sentences.zh: 但以爵位尊顯，專總權柄，天道惡盈，不知自損，故至顛仆。

translations.literal: Only because rank and position are honored and manifest, they monopolize and grasp the handles of power; Heaven’s way hates excess, and they do not know to diminish themselves; therefore they reach overturn and fall.
translations.idiomatic: It is because high rank and visible power tempt them to monopolize authority; Heaven abhors excess, yet they refuse to pull back, and so they topple.

sentences.zh: 先帝寵遇閻氏，位號太疾，故其受禍，曾不旋時。

translations.literal: The late emperor favored and encountered the Yan clan; titles and ranks came too swiftly; therefore they received disaster, in less than the time of a turning.
translations.idiomatic: The late emperor showered favor on the Yan family and raised them to lofty titles overnight; their fall came just as suddenly.

sentences.zh: 老子曰：「其進銳，其退速也。

translations.literal: Laozi says: “When its advance is sharp, its retreat is swift.”
translations.idiomatic: The Laozi warns that what shoots up fast collapses just as quickly.

sentences.zh: 」[一]今梁氏戚為椒房，禮所不臣，[二]尊以高爵，尚可然也。

translations.literal: ” [Note 1] Now the Liang clan relatives are in the pepper chambers; by ritual they are not to be served as ministers, [note 2] honoring them with high rank may still be acceptable.
translations.idiomatic: ” The Liang consort’s kin now occupy the inner palace quarters whom ritual does not treat as subjects; granting them exalted titles might still be defended.

sentences.zh: 而子弟腢從，榮顯兼加，永平﹑建初故事，殆不如此。

translations.literal: Yet their sons and younger male kin follow them in crowds, honors pile on honors, and even the precedents of the Yongping and Jianchu eras hardly matched this excess.
translations.idiomatic: But when their sons and every hanger-on in the clan rake in honors on top of honors, the court exceeds anything tolerated in the Yongping or Jianchu reigns.

sentences.zh: 宜令步兵校尉冀及諸侍中還居黃門之官，使權去外戚，政歸國家，豈不休乎！

translations.literal: It is fitting to order Colonel of Footsoldiers Ji and the various palace attendants to return and dwell in Yellow Gate offices, causing power to leave the outer kin and government to return to the state—how would this not be excellent!
translations.idiomatic: Order Colonel of the Guard Liang Ji and the other palace attendants back to the proper eunuch offices, strip outside relatives of real power, and restore policy to the public organs of state—could any reform be more timely?

sentences.zh: 注[一]案：孟子有此文。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: investigation: the Mencius has this text.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 1: the same wording appears in the Mencius.

sentences.zh: 謝承書亦云孟子，而續漢書復雲老子。

translations.literal: Xie Cheng’s book also says Mencius, but the Continuation of the Han Documents again says Laozi.
translations.idiomatic: Xie Cheng cites Mencius, whereas the Continuation of the Han Documents attributes the line to Laozi.

sentences.zh: 注[二]公羊傳曰：「宋殺其大夫，何以不名？

translations.literal: Comment [2] the Gongyang Commentary states: “Song killed its great officer—why is he not named?”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 2: the Gongyang Commentary asks why the Spring and Autumn Annals omits the minister’s personal name when recording that Song executed him.

sentences.zh: 宋三世無大夫，三世內娶也。

translations.literal: Song for three generations had no great officers; for three generations it took wives within [the state].
translations.idiomatic: Because Song had gone three generations without a true ministerial class—three generations of marrying daughters of its own grandees.

sentences.zh: 」何休注云：「內娶，娶大夫女也。

translations.literal: ” He Xiu’s commentary states: “Inner taking of wives means taking the daughters of great officers.”
translations.idiomatic: ” He Xiu explains “marrying inward” as taking wives from ministerial families inside the state.

sentences.zh: 言無大夫者三世，禮不臣妻之父母，國內皆臣，無娶道，故絕去大夫名，正其義也。

translations.literal: Meaning that for three generations there were no great officers; by ritual one does not serve as minister to one’s wife’s parents; within the state all are ministers; there is no way to take wives; therefore they cut away the great officer’s name, rectifying the meaning.
translations.idiomatic: For three generations every powerful lineage was tied by marriage to the ducal house, so ritual could not treat a wife’s father as a subject; with no “outside” families left to marry, the Annals simply drops the victim’s name to mark the moral anomaly.

sentences.zh: 」椒房者，皇后所居，以椒泥塗也。

translations.literal: ” “Pepper chambers” is where the empress dwells; it is smeared with pepper mud.
translations.idiomatic: ” The “pepper chambers” are the empress’s apartments, their walls plastered with a spiced mortar that was thought both fragrant and protective.

sentences.zh: 又詔書所以禁侍中尚書中臣子弟不得為吏察孝廉者，以其秉威權，容請托故也。

translations.literal: Moreover the reason edicts forbid palace attendants, Masters of Writing, and inner-court officials’ sons and younger brothers from becoming clerks or being investigated as filial and incorrupt is because they grasp awesome power and tolerate requests and entrustments.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial rescripts bar the sons of palace attendants and Masters of Writing from holding clerkships or receiving “filial and incorrupt” nominations precisely because those offices already wield enough patronage to invite every kind of favor-brokerage.

sentences.zh: 而中常侍日月之側，聲埶振天下，子弟祿仕，曾無限極。

translations.literal: Yet the Regular Palace Attendants are at the side of sun and moon; their voice and momentum shake the world; sons and younger brothers receive salary and office without any limit or extreme.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile the Regular Palace Attendants stand beside the throne as if they were the sun and moon themselves; their influence shakes the empire, yet their kinsmen take post after post with no ceiling at all.

sentences.zh: 雖外托謙默，不干州郡，而諂偽之徒，望風進舉。

translations.literal: Although outwardly they entrust themselves to modest silence and do not interfere with provinces and commanderies, yet fawning and false persons, catching the wind, advance and are recommended.
translations.idiomatic: They may protest humility and swear they never meddle in provincial appointments, but every sycophant in the realm still reads their mood and pushes their protégés forward.

sentences.zh: 今可為設常禁，同之中臣。

translations.literal: Now it is possible to establish for them a standing prohibition, the same as for inner-court officials.
translations.idiomatic: Extend to them the same standing prohibitions that already bind the inner court.

sentences.zh: 昔館陶公主為子求郎，[一]明帝不許，賜錢千萬。

translations.literal: Formerly the Princess of Guantao for her son sought a gentleman rank; [note 1] Ming-di did not grant it and bestowed cash of ten million.
translations.idiomatic: When the Princess of Guantao once asked Ming-di to make her son a gentleman cadet, he refused the office but gave her ten million cash instead.

sentences.zh: 所以輕厚賜，重薄位者，為官人失才，害及百姓也。

translations.literal: The reason he slighted a thick bestowal and valued a thin position was because if officials lose talent, harm reaches the hundred surnames.
translations.idiomatic: He preferred a lavish gift to a hollow title because misplacing talent in office injures the common people.

sentences.zh: 竊聞長水司馬武宣﹑[二]開陽城門候羊迪等，[三]無它功德，初拜便真。

translations.literal: I have privately heard that Chief Clerk of the Ever-Flowing Waters Wu Xuan, [note 2] Gate Warden of the Kaiyang city gate Yang Di, and others, [note 3] without other merit or power, upon first appointment then held substantive rank.
translations.idiomatic: I hear that Wu Xuan, chief clerk under the Colonel of the Long River encampment, and Yang Di, warden of the Kaiyang city gate, have no record of merit yet received substantive appointments the day they were first named.

sentences.zh: 此雖小失，而漸壞舊章。

translations.literal: Although this is a small fault, it gradually ruins the old regulations.
translations.idiomatic: Small breaches like these slowly erode the statutes that governed better times.

sentences.zh: [四]先聖法度，所宜堅守，政教一跌，百年不復。

translations.literal: [Note 4] The laws and measures of the former sages ought to be firmly guarded; if government and instruction once stumble, in a hundred years they are not restored.
translations.idiomatic: The models left by the sages deserve iron fidelity; once policy and moral instruction slip, a century may not put them right.

sentences.zh: 詩云：「上帝板板，下民卒癉。

translations.literal: The Classic of Poetry says: “High God is contrary; the lower people are utterly sick.”
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Poetry says, “Heaven’s ways are perverse, and the people below are worn to the bone with affliction.”

sentences.zh: 」刺周王變祖法度，故使下民將盡病也。

translations.literal: ” It criticizes the Zhou king for altering the ancestral laws and measures, therefore causing the lower people almost to be entirely sick.
translations.idiomatic: ” The ode blames a Zhou king who abandoned his forebears’ institutions and left the populace diseased with misery.

sentences.zh: [五] 注[一]館陶公主，光武第三女也。

translations.literal: [Note 5] Comment [1]: the Princess of Guantao was Guangwu’s third daughter.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5. Commentary, note 1: the Princess of Guantao was Emperor Guangwu’s third daughter.

sentences.zh: 注[二]續漢志「長水校尉一人，比二千石，司馬一人，千石，掌宿□」也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] the Continuation of the Han Monograph: “Colonel of the Ever-Flowing Waters, one man, rank equivalent to two thousand piculs; Chief Clerk, one man, one thousand piculs, in charge of night [text damaged].”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 2: the Later Han offices treatise lists the colonel at two thousand piculs and his chief clerk at one thousand, responsible for camp security [one character missing].

sentences.zh: 注[三]續漢志曰：「城門每門候一人，六百石。」

translations.literal: Comment [3] the Continuation of the Han Monograph states: “For city gates, each gate has one warden, six hundred piculs.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 3: the offices treatise assigns one warden at six hundred piculs to each city gate.

sentences.zh: 注[四]續漢書曰：「中都官，千石﹑六百石，故事先守一歲，然後補真。」

translations.literal: Comment [4] the Continuation of the Han Documents states: “For capital officials of one thousand piculs and six hundred piculs, by precedent they first hold probation one year, thereafter they are supplemented as substantive.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 4: capital officials at those grades were supposed to serve a probationary year before receiving substantive appointment.

sentences.zh: 注[五]板，反也。

translations.literal: Comment [5]: ban means contrary.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 5: ban here means “contrary” or “perverse.”

sentences.zh: 卒，盡也。

translations.literal: zu means exhaust.
translations.idiomatic: Zu means “utterly” or “to the limit.”

sentences.zh: 癉，病也。

translations.literal: dan means sickness.
translations.idiomatic: Dan means “affliction” or “wasting illness.”

sentences.zh: 詩大雅，凡伯刺周厲王反先王之道，下人盡病也。

translations.literal: Greater Odes of the Classic of Poetry; Fan Bo criticizes King Li of Zhou for opposing the way of the former kings; the lower people are entirely sick.
translations.idiomatic: The line comes from the Major Odes: Fan Bo reproaches King Li of Zhou for abandoning the old kings’ way and driving his subjects into misery.

sentences.zh: 今陛下之有尚書，猶天之有北斗也。

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty’s having the Masters of Writing is like Heaven’s having the Northern Dipper.
translations.idiomatic: The Masters of Writing at your court are what the Northern Dipper is to the sky.

sentences.zh: 斗為天喉舌，尚書亦為陛下喉舌。

translations.literal: The Dipper is Heaven’s throat and tongue; the Masters of Writing also are Your Majesty’s throat and tongue.
translations.idiomatic: Astrologers call the Dipper heaven’s mouthpiece; the Masters of Writing serve the same office for you.

sentences.zh: [一]斗斟酌元氣，運平四時。

translations.literal: [Note 1] The Dipper ladles and pours primordial qi and moves and balances the four seasons.
translations.idiomatic: The Dipper is said to dip the primordial breath and set the four seasons in their course.

sentences.zh: [二]尚書出納王命，賦政四海，[三]權尊埶重，責之所歸。

translations.literal: [Note 2] The Masters of Writing issue and receive the king’s commands and distribute government to the four seas; [note 3] authority is honored and momentum is heavy; responsibility returns there.
translations.idiomatic: The Masters of Writing take in and promulgate the royal commands and broadcast policy to the four seas; their authority is immense, and blame for failure settles on them.

sentences.zh: 若不平心，□眚必至。

translations.literal: If they are not even in heart, [damaged] calamity will arrive.
translations.idiomatic: If they do not judge with perfect impartiality, disaster [one character missing in the text] will follow.

sentences.zh: 誠宜審擇其人，以毗聖政。

translations.literal: Truly it is fitting carefully to select their persons, in order to assist sagely government.
translations.idiomatic: Choose the incumbents with extreme care so they can truly support enlightened rule.

sentences.zh: 今與陛下共理天下者，外則公卿尚書，內則常侍黃門，譬猶一門之內，一家之事，安則共其福慶，危則通其禍敗。

translations.literal: Now those who with Your Majesty jointly order the world: without are the excellencies, ministers, and Masters of Writing; within are the Regular Attendants and Yellow Gates; it is like within one gate, one household’s affair—when peaceful then together they share its blessing and felicity; when endangered then together they communicate its misfortune and defeat.
translations.idiomatic: Those who govern with you—the Three Excellencies and the Masters of Writing outside, the Regular Attendants and Yellow Gates inside—are like a single household behind one gate: in good times they share the credit, in crisis they sink together.

sentences.zh: 刺史﹑二千石，外統職事，內受法則。

translations.literal: Inspectors and two-thousand-piculs, without they order duties and affairs; within they receive laws and models.
translations.idiomatic: Regional inspectors and commandery governors direct public business abroad and take their standards from the capital at home.

sentences.zh: 夫表曲者景必邪，源清者流必絜，猶叩樹本，百枝皆動也。

translations.literal: When the gnomon is crooked, the shadow must be awry; when the source is clear, the flow must be pure; it is like knocking the tree root—hundred branches all move.
translations.idiomatic: When the post is bent the shadow falls awry; when the headwaters are clear the stream runs clean—strike the root and every branch trembles.

sentences.zh: 周頌曰：「薄言振之，莫不震疊。

translations.literal: The Zhou Hymns state: “Slightly speaking, when one rouses them, none do not tremble and fold.”
translations.idiomatic: The Zhou hymns say that when the ruler gives the word, none beneath him fails to tremble and submit.

sentences.zh: 」[四]此言動之於內，而應於外者也。

translations.literal: ” [Note 4] this statement means that when there is movement within, there is response without.
translations.idiomatic: ” The point is that what stirs within the palace is felt instantly in the realm beyond.

translations.literal: Asterisk [editorial mark in the received text].
translations.idiomatic: Editorial asterisk marking a break or lacuna in the commentary tradition.

translations.literal: (Still / likewise [variant gloss supplied by the editor]).
translations.idiomatic: Editorial gloss suggesting the particle should read “still” or “likewise.”

sentences.zh: **[由]*此言之，本朝號令，豈可蹉跌？

translations.literal: By this speaking, this court’s commands and orders—how can they stumble?
translations.idiomatic: On that showing, can the edicts that issue from this court afford the slightest stumble?

sentences.zh: 閒隙一開，則邪人動心；

translations.literal: Once a crevice opens, then wicked persons’ hearts move.
translations.idiomatic: Once you open the smallest crack, every schemer’s ambition begins to stir.

sentences.zh: 利競暫啟，則仁義道塞。

translations.literal: When profit and rivalry briefly open, then the way of benevolence and rightness is blocked.
translations.idiomatic: Let men scramble briefly for private gain and the public way of benevolence and duty is blocked.

sentences.zh: 刑罰不能復禁，化導以之寑壞。

translations.literal: Punishments and fines cannot again restrain; transformation and guidance by this are gradually ruined.
translations.idiomatic: Punishments will no longer hold men in check, and the moral guidance of government quietly rots away.

sentences.zh: 此天下之紀綱，當今之急務。

translations.literal: This is the world’s cords and net; it is the present age’s urgent task.
translations.idiomatic: These are the cables that hold the empire together, and they demand your most urgent attention.

sentences.zh: 陛下宜開石室，陳圖書，[五]招會腢儒，引問失得，指擿變象，以求天意。

translations.literal: Your Majesty ought to open the stone chambers, spread out charts and documents, [note 5] summon and assemble the scholars, draw them in and ask about loss and gain, point out and expose changing portents, in order to seek Heaven’s intention.
translations.idiomatic: Open the imperial archives, spread out the charts and classics, convene the Confucian scholars, press them on what has gone wrong, lay every omen before them, and so read Heaven’s will.

sentences.zh: 其言有中理，實時施行，顯拔其人，以表能者。

translations.literal: If their words hit upon principle, in fact timely implement them, conspicuously raise those persons, in order to display the able.
translations.idiomatic: When their advice rings true, put it into practice at once and promote the speakers themselves as models of competence.

sentences.zh: 則聖聽日有所聞，忠臣盡其所知。

translations.literal: Then sagely hearing daily has something to hear, and loyal ministers exhaust what they know.
translations.idiomatic: Your ears will daily receive fresh counsel, and loyal officials will lay every thought before you.

sentences.zh: 又宜罷退宦官，去其權重，裁置常侍二人，方直有德者，省事左右；

translations.literal: Moreover it is fitting to dismiss and retire the palace eunuchs, remove their weight of power, reduce and set Regular Attendants at two men, square and straight, possessing virtue, to simplify affairs at left and right.
translations.idiomatic: You should also dismiss the bulk of the inner eunuchs, strip their power down to two Regular Attendants of proven integrity to handle routine business at your side.

sentences.zh: 小黃門五人，才智閑雅者，給事殿中。

translations.literal: five Junior Yellow Gates, those whose talent and wisdom are refined and elegant, to supply service in the palace halls.
translations.idiomatic: Assign five Junior Yellow Gates, men of calm ability, to serve inside the audience halls.

sentences.zh: 如此，則論者厭塞，昇平可致也。

translations.literal: Like this, then those who discourse will be satisfied and blocked [i.e., critics silenced], and rising peace can be brought about.
translations.idiomatic: Do this, and carping at court will die away while the age itself can climb back toward peace.

sentences.zh: 臣所以敢陳愚瞽，冒昧自聞者，儻或皇天欲令微臣覺悟陛下。

translations.literal: The reason your servant dares to present foolish blindness and risk rudeness to make himself heard is perhaps that August Heaven wishes to cause the petty minister to awaken Your Majesty.
translations.idiomatic: I risk this blunt memorial because Heaven itself may intend a minor official to wake Your Majesty to the danger.

sentences.zh: 陛下宜熟察臣言，憐赦臣死。

translations.literal: Your Majesty ought maturely to investigate your servant’s words and pityingly pardon your servant’s death.
translations.idiomatic: Read these words with care, and in your mercy spare my life for having spoken them.

sentences.zh: 注[一]春秋合誠圖曰：「天理在斗中，司三公，如人喉在咽，以理舌語。

translations.literal: Comment [1] the Spring and Autumn Hequ and Sincerity Chart states: “Heaven’s pattern is in the Dipper; it governs the Three Dukes; it is like man’s throat in the pharynx, by which to order tongue and speech.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 1: an apocryphon on the Spring and Autumn Annals places Heaven’s pattern in the Dipper, which “manages the Three Dukes” as the human throat governs tongue and speech.

sentences.zh: 」宋均注曰：「斗為天之舌口，主出政教。

translations.literal: ” Song Jun’s commentary states: “The Dipper is Heaven’s tongue and mouth; it governs issuing government and instruction.”
translations.idiomatic: ” Song Jun glosses the Dipper as Heaven’s mouthpiece, issuing edicts and moral instruction.

sentences.zh: 三公主導宣君命，喻於人，則宜如人喉在咽，以理舌口，使言有條理。」

translations.literal: The Three Dukes govern and declare the lord’s commands; in analogy to man, it is fitting to be like man’s throat in the pharynx, by which to order tongue and mouth, causing speech to have order and pattern.”
translations.idiomatic: The Three Dukes proclaim the ruler’s will to mankind, just as the throat orders tongue and lips so that speech comes out coherently.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]春秋保干圖曰：「天皇於是斟元陳樞，以五易威。

translations.literal: Comment [2] the Spring and Autumn Protecting the Stem Chart states: “The Heavenly Emperor thereupon ladles primordial and arrays the pivot, by the five changes authority.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 2: another apocryphon describes the Heavenly Emperor ladling primordial qi and setting the cosmic pivot so that the five phases rotate authority.

sentences.zh: 」宋均注曰：「威，則也，法也。

translations.literal: ” Song Jun’s commentary states: “Wei means rule; it means model.”
translations.idiomatic: ” Song Jun reads wei as “normative pattern” or “law.”

sentences.zh: 天皇斟元氣，陳列樞機，受行次之當得也。」

translations.literal: The Heavenly Emperor ladles primordial qi, arrays and lists the pivot mechanism, receiving and proceeding in sequence of what ought to be obtained.”
translations.idiomatic: The god ladles out primordial breath, sets the cosmic gears in order, and assigns each thing its proper turn in the sequence.”

sentences.zh: 注[三]賦，布也。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: fu means spread.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 3: fu means “to spread abroad” or “publish.”

sentences.zh: 注[四]韓詩□君傳曰：「薄，辭也。

translations.literal: Comment [4] the Han exoteric poem tradition’s Lord Xue commentary states: “Bo is a particle of speech.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 4: the Han-school gloss on the Mao poem explains bo as a function word.

sentences.zh: 振，奮也。

translations.literal: Zhen means rouse.
translations.idiomatic: Zhen means “to rouse” or “stir into action.”

sentences.zh: 莫，無也。

translations.literal: Mo means none.
translations.idiomatic: Mo means “none” or “no one.”

sentences.zh: 震，動也。

translations.literal: Zhen means move.
translations.idiomatic: Zhen means “to tremble” or “be shaken.”

sentences.zh: 疊，應也。

translations.literal: Die means respond.
translations.idiomatic: Die means “to answer” or “respond in kind.”

sentences.zh: 美成王能奮舒文武之道而行之，則天下無不動而應其政教。」

translations.literal: It praises King Cheng for being able to rouse and unfold the way of Wen and Wu and practice it; then none under Heaven fail to move and respond to his government and instruction.”
translations.idiomatic: It praises King Cheng for reviving the policies of Kings Wen and Wu so thoroughly that the whole realm stirred in answer to his rule.”

sentences.zh: 注[五]前書曰：「司馬遷為太史令，紬史記石室金匱之書。

translations.literal: Comment [5] the Former Han Documents states: “Sima Qian was Grand Astrologer; he drew out the Records of the Grand Historian from the stone chamber and metal coffer books.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 5: the Han shu says Sima Qian, as Grand Astrologer, compiled the Shiji from texts kept in the imperial stone archive and metal coffer.

sentences.zh: 」紬音抽。

translations.literal: ” The graph chou is read like chou “to draw out.”
translations.idiomatic: The commentary glosses chou with the “draw out” reading.

sentences.zh: 順帝覽其對，多所納用，實時出阿母還弟捨，諸常侍悉叩頭謝罪，朝廷肅然。

translations.literal: Emperor Shun read his reply, much adopted and used it; at that time he sent the nurse-nanny out to return to her younger brother’s house; the various Regular Attendants all knocked their heads apologizing for guilt; the court was solemn.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Shun read Li Gu’s memorial, adopted much of it, and at once sent the wet-nurse Wang Sheng back to her brother’s house; the Regular Attendants kowtowed in acknowledgment of fault, and the whole court stood in awe.

sentences.zh: 以固為議郎。

translations.literal: They appointed Gu as Gentleman Consultant.
translations.idiomatic: The court named Li Gu Gentleman Consultant.

sentences.zh: 而阿母宦者疾固言直，因詐飛章以陷其罪，事從中下。

translations.literal: But the nurse-nanny and the palace eunuchs hated Gu’s words being straight; thereupon they forged a flying memorial to trap him in crime; the affair came down from the center.
translations.idiomatic: The wet-nurse and her eunuch allies resented his blunt speech and fabricated an anonymous denunciation; an order came down from the palace before any open deliberation.

sentences.zh: 大司農黃尚等請之於大將軍梁商，又僕射黃瓊救明固事，久乃得拜議郎。

translations.literal: Grand Minister of Agriculture Huang Shang and others pleaded for him with Grand General Liang Shang, and also the Supervisor Huang Qiong clarified Gu’s case; after a long time he then obtained appointment as Gentleman Consultant.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Shang, Minister of Agriculture, interceded with Grand General Liang Shang, while Huang Qiong, Vice Director of the Secretariat, cleared Li Gu’s name, and only after prolonged effort was he again confirmed as Gentleman Consultant.

sentences.zh: 注[三]春秋隱公二年，經書「無駭帥師入極」。

translations.literal: Comment [3] the second year of Duke Yin in the Spring and Autumn Annals, the classic records “Wu Hai led the army into Ji.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 3: for Duke Yin’s second year the Annals record that Wu Hai led troops into the state of Ji.

sentences.zh: 公羊傳曰：「無駭者何？

translations.literal: The Gongyang Commentary states: “What is Wu Hai?
translations.idiomatic: The Gongyang Commentary asks: “Who is this Wu Hai?”

sentences.zh: 展無駭也。

translations.literal: He is Zhan Wu Hai.
translations.idiomatic: The man is Zhan Wu Hai of the house of Zhan.

sentences.zh: 何以不氏？

translations.literal: Why is his clan not used?
translations.idiomatic: Why does the text withhold his clan name?

sentences.zh: 貶。

translations.literal: Deprecation.
translations.idiomatic: The Annals are expressing censure.

sentences.zh: 曷為貶？

translations.literal: Why is it deprecation?
translations.idiomatic: Why does the classic blame him?

sentences.zh: 疾始滅也。」

translations.literal: Because it abhors the beginning of extinguishing [other states].”
translations.idiomatic: Because the classic condemns the first step toward wiping out another state.”

sentences.zh: 注[四]伯榮，王聖女也。

translations.literal: Comment [4]: Bo Rong is Wang Sheng’s daughter.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 4: Bo Rong was the daughter of Wang Sheng.

sentences.zh: 注[五]守死善道，論語文。

translations.literal: Comment [5]: “Guard unto death the good way”—language from the Analects.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 5: the phrase “cling to the right path even unto death” comes from the Analects.

sentences.zh: 滯涸窮路，以魚為諭也。

translations.literal: Stagnant and dried up on a hopeless road—it uses fish as a metaphor.
translations.idiomatic: The image is of a fish stranded as the stream dries up—a figure for a man trapped at the end of his options.

sentences.zh: 注[六]趙飛燕，成帝皇后。

translations.literal: Comment [6]: Zhao Feiyan was Emperor Cheng’s empress.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 6: Zhao Feiyan, empress to Emperor Cheng of Western Han.

sentences.zh: 妹為昭儀，專寵。

translations.literal: Her younger sister became Brilliant Companion and monopolized favor.
translations.idiomatic: Her sister became Brilliant Companion and together they held the emperor’s exclusive favor.

sentences.zh: 成帝貴人曹偉能等生皇子，皆殺之。

translations.literal: Emperor Cheng’s noblewomen such as Cao Weineng bore imperial sons; all were killed.
translations.idiomatic: When lesser consorts such as Cao Weineng gave birth to imperial sons, the sisters saw the infants murdered.

sentences.zh: 注[七]雲起貌。

translations.literal: Comment [7]: the appearance of clouds rising.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 7: the graph describes cloudbanks piling upward.

sentences.zh: 注[八]祗，敬也。

translations.literal: Comment [8]: zhi means reverence.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 8: zhi means “to revere” or “stand in awe.”

sentences.zh: 言天無親疏，惟善是與，可敬*

translations.literal: It means Heaven shows no partiality between near and kin; it sides only with the good; one should stand in awe [text damaged].
translations.idiomatic: Heaven does not favor kin over strangers; it aids only the virtuous—hence the sage’s awe [one character damaged in the manuscript].

translations.literal: (Variant gloss: awe / majesty.)
translations.idiomatic: Some editions gloss the damaged graph as “majesty” rather than “awe.”

translations.literal: **[It means awe / reverence.]**
translations.idiomatic: The restored reading is “to hold in awe.”

sentences.zh: 書曰：「皇天無親。」

translations.literal: The Documents state: “High God is not partial.”
translations.idiomatic: The Shang shu says, “August Heaven shows no favoritism.”

sentences.zh: 注[九]既，盡也。

translations.literal: Comment [9]: ji means exhaust.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 9: ji means “utterly” or “to the end.”

sentences.zh: 端門，太微宮南門也。

translations.literal: The Duan Gate is the south gate of the Grand Tenuity palace [in the celestial bureaucracy].
translations.idiomatic: The Duan Gate is identified with the southern portal of the Grand Tenuity constellation in astrological lore.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]前書李尋上疏曰：「月者觿陰之長，妃後﹑大臣﹑諸侯之象也。」

translations.literal: Comment [10] the Former Han Documents records Li Xun’s memorial: “The moon is the chief of extreme yin; it is the image of consorts, great ministers, and feudal lords.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 10: Li Xun told Emperor Ai that the moon symbolizes yin at its peak and stands for empresses, chief ministers, and regional lords.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]易豐卦曰：「日中則昃，月盈則食，天地盈虛，與時消息。

translations.literal: Comment [11] the Book of Changes, Feng hexagram, says: “When the sun stands at noon it begins to decline; when the moon is full it begins to wane; Heaven and Earth wax and wane with the seasons, ebbing and flowing.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 11: the Zhou yi’s Feng hexagram warns that noon turns toward dusk and a full moon toward eclipse—cosmic fullness alternates with emptiness.

sentences.zh: 」史記蔡澤謂范睢曰：「日中則移，月滿則虧」也。

translations.literal: ” The Records of the Grand Historian has Cai Ze say to Fan Ju: “When the sun is at noon it shifts; when the moon is full it wanes.”
translations.idiomatic: ” Sima Qian’s biography of Fan Ju quotes Cai Ze’s warning that every zenith carries the seed of decline.

sentences.zh: 注[一二]易曰：「鬼神害盈而福謙，人道惡盈而好謙。

translations.literal: Comment [12] the Changes says: “Spirits and gods injure fullness and bless humility; the human way hates fullness and loves humility.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 12: the Zhou yi says spirits punish arrogance and reward modesty, and human affairs follow the same pattern.

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「見天地之心。」

translations.literal: ” It also says: “Thus one sees the mind of Heaven and Earth.”
translations.idiomatic: ” Another line from the same tradition says the humble line reveals “the heart of Heaven and Earth.”

sentences.zh: 注[一三]老子曰：「功成名遂身退，天之道也。」

translations.literal: Comment [13] Laozi says: “When merit is achieved and name secured, withdraw the body—this is Heaven’s way.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 13: the Laozi teaches that once your work is done and your name made, step back—that is the Dao of Heaven.

sentences.zh: 注[一四]為利所誘，怵迫於憂勤也。

translations.literal: Comment [14]: enticed by profit, pressed by worry and toil.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 14: the sage warns against letting gain lure you into frantic anxiety.

sentences.zh: 怵音息律反，或音黜。

translations.literal: Chu is read with the fanqie syllables xi-lü; some read it as chu.
translations.idiomatic: The commentary gives the fanqie spelling for chu; some readers prefer the alternate pronunciation chu.

sentences.zh: 注[一五]莊子曰：「伯成子高，唐虞時為諸侯，至禹，去而耕。

translations.literal: Comment [15] Zhuangzi says: “Bo Cheng Zigao was a feudal lord in the age of Yao and Shun; when Yu came to the throne he resigned and plowed the fields.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 15: Zhuangzi tells how Bo Cheng Zigao gave up his fief when Yu succeeded Shun and went off to farm.

sentences.zh: 禹往見之，則耕在野。

translations.literal: Yu went to see him and found him plowing in the wilds.
translations.idiomatic: Yu found him behind the plow, far from any court.

sentences.zh: 禹問曰：『昔堯化天下，吾子立為諸侯，堯授舜，舜授予，子去而耕，其故何也？

translations.literal: Yu asked: “Formerly Yao ordered the realm; you stood as a feudal lord; Yao yielded to Shun, Shun yielded to me—why did you leave and plow?”
translations.idiomatic: Yu asked why he had abandoned his title now that Yao’s and Shun’s mandate had passed to Yu himself.

sentences.zh: 』子高曰：『昔堯化天下，至公無私，不賞而人自勸，不罰而人自畏。

translations.literal: ” Zigao said: “Formerly Yao ordered the realm with utmost impartiality; without rewards the people urged themselves on; without punishments the people stood in awe.”
translations.idiomatic: ” Zigao answered that under Yao the world needed neither bribes nor threats because the ruler’s fairness alone moved the people.

sentences.zh: 今子賞而不勸，罰而不威，德自此衰，刑自此作。

translations.literal: “Now you rely on rewards yet the people are not stirred; you rely on punishments yet they feel no awe; from this virtue declines and punishments multiply.”
translations.idiomatic: “Under your rule,” he told Yu, “rewards no longer inspire and punishments no longer frighten; virtue withers while penal law spreads.”

sentences.zh: 夫子盍行，無留吾事。

translations.literal: “Sir, you had better go on your way—do not interrupt my work.”
translations.idiomatic: “Be on your way,” he said; “do not disturb my plowing.”

sentences.zh: 』俋俋然，耕不顧。

translations.literal: ” Stolidly he plowed and would not look up.
translations.idiomatic: ” He bent to his furrows and would not raise his eyes.

sentences.zh: 」亦見呂氏春秋。

translations.literal: ” The story also appears in the Lüshi chunqiu.
translations.idiomatic: ” A parallel version appears in the Lüshi chunqiu.

sentences.zh: 注[一六]謂靈輒也。

translations.literal: Comment [16]: it refers to Ling Zhe.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 16: the allusion is to the retainer Ling Zhe.

sentences.zh: 永和中，荊州盜賊起，彌年不定，乃以固為荊州刺史。

translations.literal: In the Yonghe era bandits arose in Jing Province and could not be pacified for a full year; thereupon they appointed Gu as Inspector of Jing Province.
translations.idiomatic: During the Yonghe period (136–141) Jing Province erupted in banditry that dragged on for a year, so the court sent Li Gu as regional inspector.

sentences.zh: 固到，遣吏勞問境內，赦寇盜前釁，與之更始。

translations.literal: When Gu arrived he sent clerks to console the commanderies and absolved the bandits of prior offenses, giving them a fresh start.
translations.idiomatic: On arrival he sent officials through the region with word of amnesty, wiping the slate clean for anyone who would submit.

sentences.zh: 於是賊帥夏密等斂其魁黨六百餘人，自縛歸首。

translations.literal: Thereupon bandit leaders such as Xia Mi gathered more than six hundred chiefs of their gangs, bound themselves, and came to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: Chieftains such as Xia Mi then brought forward six hundred-odd ringleaders in fetters to yield at the yamen.

sentences.zh: 固皆原之，遣還，使自相招集，開示威法。

translations.literal: Gu pardoned them all, sent them back, and had them summon their followers while publishing clear laws of reward and awe.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu released them, sent them home to rally their followers, and published generous terms backed by firm law.

sentences.zh: 半歲閒，余類悉降，州內清平。

translations.literal: Within half a year the remaining bands all submitted, and the province was clear and calm.
translations.idiomatic: Within six months the rest had capitulated and the province was quiet.

sentences.zh: 上奏南陽太守高賜等臧穢。

translations.literal: He memorialized to impeach the Administrator of Nanyang, Gao Ci, and others for corruption.
translations.idiomatic: He filed charges against Gao Ci, administrator of Nanyang, and other officials for bribery and extortion.

sentences.zh: 賜等懼罪，遂共重賂大將軍梁冀，冀為千里移檄，[一] 而固持之愈急。

translations.literal: Ci and the others feared punishment and together sent heavy bribes to Grand General Liang Ji; Ji for them moved a proclamation a thousand li in a day [note 1], yet Gu pressed the case all the harder.
translations.idiomatic: Gao Ci and his allies, terrified of conviction, pooled a huge bribe for Grand General Liang Ji, who fired off an urgent rescript across a thousand li, but Li Gu only tightened the investigation.

sentences.zh: 冀遂令徙固為太山太守。

translations.literal: Ji thereupon ordered Gu transferred to be Administrator of Taishan.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji then had him reassigned as governor of Taishan commandery.

sentences.zh: 時太山盜賊屯聚歷年，郡兵常千人，追討不能制。

translations.literal: At that time Taishan bandits had gathered for years; the commandery troops, normally a thousand men, pursued and attacked but could not control them.
translations.idiomatic: Taishan had long been overrun by gangs that a standing force of a thousand local troops could not subdue.

sentences.zh: 固到，悉罷遣歸農，但選留任戰者百餘人，以恩信招誘之。

translations.literal: When Gu arrived he dismissed them all to return to farming, keeping only a bit more than a hundred men fit for battle, and with kindness and trust summoned and lured the bandits.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu disbanded the militia and sent the farmers home, retaining barely a hundred seasoned fighters, then won the bandits over with mercy and good faith.

sentences.zh: 未滿歲，賊皆弭散。

translations.literal: Before a full year had passed the bandits all dispersed.
translations.idiomatic: Inside a year the outlaw bands had melted away.

sentences.zh: 注[一]言移一日行千里，救之急也。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: it means moving a proclamation a thousand li in one day—the urgency of rescue.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 1: the phrase pictures a courier galloping a thousand li in a day to rescue an ally.

sentences.zh: 遷將作大匠。

translations.literal: He was promoted to Court Architect.
translations.idiomatic: He was promoted to Court Architect, superintendent of imperial works.

sentences.zh: 上疏陳事曰：「臣聞氣之清者為神，人之清者為賢。

translations.literal: He submitted a memorial stating: “Your servant has heard that what is clear in qi becomes spirit, and what is clear in a man becomes worthiness.”
translations.idiomatic: He presented a memorial: “I have heard that the subtlest breath becomes numinous power, and the clearest human character becomes true worth.”

sentences.zh: 養身者以練神為寶，安國者以積賢為道。

translations.literal: Those who cultivate the body take refining the spirit as treasure; those who pacify the state take accumulating the worthy as the way.
translations.idiomatic: Self-cultivation prizes the tempered spirit; ordering the realm depends on gathering able men around the throne.

sentences.zh: 昔秦欲謀楚，王孫圉設□西門，陳列名臣，秦使戄然，遂為寑兵。

translations.literal: Formerly Qin wished to plot against Chu; Prince Zhaoxi set up [damaged] at the west gate, arrayed famous ministers; the Qin envoy was shaken and thereupon desisted from arms.
translations.idiomatic: When Qin planned to attack Chu, Prince Zhaoxi of Chu arrayed his best ministers at the west gate; the Qin envoy took one look and dropped the campaign.

sentences.zh: [一] 魏文侯師卜子夏，友田子方，軾段干木，故腢俊競至，名過齊桓，秦人不敢窺兵於西河，斯蓋積賢人之符也。

translations.literal: [Note 1] Marquis Wen of Wei took Bu Zixia as teacher, Tian Zifang as friend, and raised the carriage bar for Duan Ganmu; therefore the worthy crowded in, his fame surpassed Duke Huan of Qi, and the people of Qin did not dare cast eyes on troops at the western River—this was surely the tally of accumulated worthies.
translations.idiomatic: Marquis Wen of Wei studied with Bu Zixia, befriended Tian Zifang, and bowed from his chariot to recluse Duan Ganmu, so talent flocked to him, his reputation outshone Duke Huan of Qi, and Qin dared not move against the western bend of the Yellow River—that is what it means to stockpile worthy men.

sentences.zh: [二]陛下撥亂龍飛，初登大位，聘南陽樊英、江夏黃瓊、廣漢楊厚、會稽賀純，[三]策書嗟歎，待以大夫之位。

translations.literal: [Note 2] Your Majesty, rising like a dragon amid chaos, first ascended the great position, and summoned Fan Ying of Nanyang, Huang Qiong of Jiangxia, Yang Hou of Guanghan, and He Chun of Kuaiji; [note 3] edicts sighed in admiration and treated them with the rank of grandees.
translations.idiomatic: When Your Majesty first took the throne you summoned Fan Ying of Nanyang, Huang Qiong of Jiangxia, Yang Hou of Guanghan, and He Chun of Kuaiji, issued edicts of praise, and received them with the ceremony due senior counselors.

sentences.zh: 是以巖穴幽人，智術之士，彈冠振衣，樂欲為用，四海欣然，歸服聖德。

translations.literal: Therefore recluses from cliffs and caves and masters of strategy shook out their caps and brushed their robes, rejoicing to be employed; within the four seas all were glad and submitted to sagely virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Hermits and strategists dusted off their caps and hurried to serve; the empire rejoiced that a sage was on the throne.

sentences.zh: 厚等在職，雖無奇卓，然夕惕孳孳，志在憂國。

translations.literal: Hou and the others in office, although they had no outstanding excellence, yet nightly feared and daily toiled, their intent set on worrying for the state.
translations.idiomatic: Those men, once in office, showed no spectacular reforms, but they worked late and rose early with the cares of the dynasty always in mind.

sentences.zh: 臣前在荊州，聞厚、純等以病免歸，誠以悵然，為時惜之。

translations.literal: Your servant, formerly in Jing Province, heard that Hou, Chun, and others because of illness were dismissed and returned home; truly he was disappointed and regretted it for the age.
translations.idiomatic: When I governed Jing Province I learned that Yang Hou and He Chun had resigned on grounds of illness, and I grieved for the court as much as for themselves.

sentences.zh: 一日朝會，見諸侍中並皆年少，無一宿儒大人可顧問者，誠可歎息。

translations.literal: One day at court meeting he saw that the various palace attendants were all young and there was not one old Confucian great man to consult—truly it was something to sigh over.
translations.idiomatic: At a recent audience every attendant at your side was a young man; not one venerable scholar stood ready for counsel—this is a sorry sight.

sentences.zh: 宜征還厚等，以副腢望。

translations.literal: It is fitting to summon back Hou and the others to answer the expectations of the throng.
translations.idiomatic: Recall Yang Hou and his like to satisfy what the empire expects of you.

sentences.zh: 瓊久處議郎，已且十年，觿人皆怪始隆崇，今更滯也。

translations.literal: Qiong long dwelt as Gentleman Consultant—already nearly ten years—and people all wondered that at first he was greatly honored and now is more stalled.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Qiong has languished as Gentleman Consultant for the better part of a decade; the court first exalted him, then left him in limbo, to everyone’s puzzlement.

sentences.zh: [四]光祿大夫周舉，才謨高正，宜在常伯，訪以言議。

translations.literal: [Note 4] Imperial Household Grandee Zhou Ju, talented in planning and lofty in rectitude, ought to be among the Regular Attendants; consult him on policy.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Ju, Grandee of the Imperial Household, is a man of high principle and sound counsel; move him to the Regular Attendants and sound him on state affairs.

sentences.zh: 侍中杜喬，學深行直，當世良臣，久托疾病，可□令起。

translations.literal: Palace Attendant Du Qiao’s learning is deep and his conduct straight—an excellent minister of the age; long he has lodged illness—[damaged] order him to rise.
translations.idiomatic: Palace Attendant Du Qiao is learned, upright, and the best minister of his generation, yet he has pleaded illness for ages; command him back to duty [one verb damaged].

sentences.zh: 」又薦陳留楊倫、[五]河南尹存、東平王惲、陳國何臨、[六]清河房植等。

translations.literal: ” He also recommended Yang Lun of Chenliu, Yin Cun of Henan, Wang Yun of Dongping, He Lin of Chen, Fang Zhi of Qinghe, and others.
translations.idiomatic: ” He further nominated Yang Lun of Chenliu, Yin Cun of Henan, Wang Yun of Dongping, He Lin of Chen, Fang Zhi of Qinghe, and others.

sentences.zh: [七]是日有詔徵用倫、厚等，而遷瓊、舉，以固為大司農。

translations.literal: [Note 7] That day there was an edict summoning and employing Lun, Hou, and others, while Qiong and Ju were promoted, and Gu was made Grand Minister of Agriculture.
translations.idiomatic: The same day an edict called Yang Lun, Yang Hou, and the rest to office, promoted Huang Qiong and Zhou Ju, and named Li Gu Grand Minister of Agriculture.

sentences.zh: 注[一]秦欲伐楚，使使者往觀楚之寶器。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Qin wished to attack Chu and sent an envoy to observe Chu’s treasures.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 1: when Qin planned to invade Chu it first sent an envoy to scout Chu’s “treasures.”

sentences.zh: 昭奚恤乃為□，使客東面，自居西面之□，稱曰：「理百姓，實倉廩，子西在此；

translations.literal: Zhao Xi then set up [damaged], had the guest face east while he himself took the seat to the west [damaged], declaring: “To order the hundred surnames and fill the granaries, Zixi is here;
translations.idiomatic: Prince Zhaoxi arranged the reception [hall layout damaged]: the envoy faced east while the prince faced west and announced: “For governing the people and filling the granaries, here is Zixi;

sentences.zh: 奉珪璋，使諸侯，子方在此；

translations.literal: to hold the jade tablets and deal with the feudal lords, Zifang is here;
translations.idiomatic: for bearing credentials to the feudal lords, here is Zifang;

sentences.zh: 守封疆，謹境界，葉公子高在此；

translations.literal: to guard the borders and watch the boundaries, Duke Ye Gao is here;
translations.idiomatic: for guarding frontiers and watching the marches, here is Duke Ye Gao;

sentences.zh: 理師旅，正兵戎，司馬子反在此；

translations.literal: to order the armies and set the hosts of war, Sima Zifan is here;
translations.idiomatic: for commanding hosts and reviewing troops, here is Marshal Zifan;

sentences.zh: 懷霸王之餘義，獵治亂之遺風，昭奚恤在此：惟大國所觀。

translations.literal: to cherish the kingly leavings of hegemony and hunt down the surviving airs of order and chaos, Zhao Xi is here—let the great state observe what it will.”
translations.idiomatic: and for the moral legacy of true kingship and the arts of peace and war, here am I, Zhao Xi—let your great state look as it pleases.”

sentences.zh: 」使反，言於秦君曰：「楚多賢臣，未可謀也。

translations.literal: ” The envoy returned and reported to the ruler of Qin: “Chu has many worthy ministers; it cannot be plotted against.”
translations.idiomatic: ” The envoy reported to the Qin king that Chu was full of able ministers and should not be attacked.

sentences.zh: 」事見新序。

translations.literal: ” The affair appears in the Xin xu.
translations.idiomatic: ” The anecdote is preserved in Liu Xiang’s Xin xu.

sentences.zh: 國語曰，楚王孫圉聘於晉，趙簡子鳴玉以相，問圉曰：「楚之白珩猶在乎，其為寶也幾何？

translations.literal: The Discourses of the States says that when Prince Zhaoyu of Chu was on embassy to Jin, Zhao Jianzi jingled his jade pendants in greeting and asked Zhaoyu: “Chu’s white hang pendant—is it still there? What is its value as a treasure?
translations.idiomatic: The Guo yu records that when Prince Zhaoyu of Chu visited Jin, Zhao Jianzi of Jin clinked his jade ornaments and asked whether Chu still treasured its famous white jade hang.

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「未嘗為寶也。

translations.literal: ” He replied: “We have never regarded it as a treasure.”
translations.idiomatic: ” The prince answered that Chu had never treated such trinkets as its real treasure.

sentences.zh: 楚人有觀射父，能作訓辭以行諸侯，有左史倚相，道訓典以序百物，此楚國之寶也。

translations.literal: The people of Chu have Guan Shefu, who can compose instructive speeches to conduct the feudal lords, and have the Left Historian Yi Xiang, who recites the normative classics to array the myriad things—these are Chu’s treasures.
translations.idiomatic: Chu’s true jewels are Guan Shefu, whose diplomatic rhetoric commands the regional lords, and Yi Xiang, the Left Historian, who expounds the canonical texts that order every affair of state.

sentences.zh: 若夫古玉、白珩，先王之所玩也，何寶焉！

translations.literal: As for ancient jade and the white hang, they are what former kings played with—how are they treasures!
translations.idiomatic: Antique jades and ceremonial pendants are playthings of dead kings—what makes them “treasures”?

sentences.zh: 」與此所引不同也。

translations.literal: ” This differs from what is quoted here.
translations.idiomatic: ” The wording differs slightly from the passage cited above.

sentences.zh: 注[二]魏文侯受經於子夏，過段干木閭，未嘗不軾也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Marquis Wen of Wei received the classics from Zixia; passing Duan Ganmu’s lane he never failed to raise the carriage bar.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 2: Marquis Wen studied the classics under Bu Zixia and always raised his chariot bar in respect when passing Duan Ganmu’s lane.

sentences.zh: 李克曰：「文侯東得卜子夏、田子方、段干木，此三人者，君皆師之。

translations.literal: Li Ke said: “East of the mountains Lord Wen obtained Bu Zixia, Tian Zifang, and Duan Ganmu—these three men the lord all took as teachers.”
translations.idiomatic: Li Ke observed that east of the Taihang range Marquis Wen had won Bu Zixia, Tian Zifang, and Duan Ganmu, each honored as a teacher.

sentences.zh: 」又秦欲伐魏，或曰：「魏君賢人是禮，國人稱仁，上下和合，未可圖也。

translations.literal: ” Also Qin wished to attack Wei; someone said: “The lord of Wei treats worthies with ritual; the people call him benevolent; high and low are in harmony—he cannot be plotted against.”
translations.idiomatic: ” When Qin later planned to strike Wei, advisers warned that the Wei ruler honored worthies, won the people’s love, and ruled a united state—no easy prey.

sentences.zh: 」事見史記也。

translations.literal: ” The affair is seen in the Records of the Grand Historian.
translations.idiomatic: ” Sima Qian records the same point.

sentences.zh: 注[三]謝承書曰：「純字仲真，會稽山陰人。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Xie Cheng’s book states: “Chun, courtesy name Zhongzhen, was a man of Shanyin in Kuaiji.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 3: Xie Cheng identifies He Chun, style Zhongzhen, as a native of Shanyin in Kuaiji.

sentences.zh: 少為諸生，博極腢蓺。

translations.literal: In youth he was a student and broadly exhausted the many arts.
translations.idiomatic: In his student days he mastered every branch of learning.

sentences.zh: 十辟公府，三舉賢良方正，五征博士，四公車征，皆不就。

translations.literal: Ten times he was summoned to the high offices’ mansions; thrice he was recommended as worthy, upright, and true; five times he was summoned as erudite; four times the public carriage summoned him—each time he did not go.
translations.idiomatic: He ignored ten summonses from the chief ministers, three nominations as “worthy and upright,” five calls to the imperial academy, and four imperial coach invitations.

sentences.zh: 後征拜議郎，數陳□異，上便宜數百事，多見省納。

translations.literal: Later he was summoned and appointed Gentleman Consultant; repeatedly he set forth [damaged] anomalies and submitted convenient measures in several hundred items, many of which were examined and adopted.
translations.idiomatic: When he finally accepted appointment as Gentleman Consultant he repeatedly reported portents [one graph damaged] and offered hundreds of practical reforms, most of which the throne accepted.

sentences.zh: 遷江夏太守。」

translations.literal: He was promoted to Administrator of Jiangxia.”
translations.idiomatic: He rose to serve as governor of Jiangxia commandery.”

sentences.zh: 注[四]隆，高也。

translations.literal: Comment [4]: long means high.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 4: long means “lofty” or “elevated.”

sentences.zh: 崇，重也。

translations.literal: Chong means weighty.
translations.idiomatic: Chong means “weighty” or “honored.”

sentences.zh: 注[五]倫見儒林傳。

translations.literal: Comment [5]: Lun appears in the Forest of Confucians treatise.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 5: Yang Lun is treated in the chapter on Confucian scholars.

sentences.zh: 注[六]臨字子陵，熙之子，為平原太守，見百家譜也。

translations.literal: Comment [6]: Lin, courtesy name Ziling, was Xi’s son; he was Administrator of Pingyuan—see the Hundred Surnames genealogy.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 6: He Lin, style Ziling, son of He Xi, served as governor of Pingyuan; the pedigree books record him.

sentences.zh: 注[七]植見黨人篇也。

translations.literal: Comment [7]: Zhi appears in the chapter on partisan worthies.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 7: Fang Zhi is discussed in the treatise on the partisan prohibitions.

sentences.zh: 先是周舉等八使案察天下，多所劾奏，其中並是宦者親屬，輒為請乞，詔遂令勿考。

translations.literal: Earlier Zhou Ju and the other eight envoys inspected the empire and submitted many impeachments; among them were also kin of the palace eunuchs, who immediately made pleas and requests, and an edict thereupon ordered that they not be investigated.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, when Zhou Ju and seven other inspectors toured the realm, their memorials implicated many eunuchs’ relatives, who lobbied the throne until an edict forbade further inquiry into those cases.

sentences.zh: 又舊任三府選令史，光祿試尚書郎，時皆特拜，不復選試。

translations.literal: Moreover, by old practice the Three Offices selected clerks and the Imperial Household tested Masters of Writing gentlemen; at this time all received special appointment and were no longer chosen by examination.
translations.idiomatic: The old rule had the Three Bureaus nominate clerks and the Imperial Household examine candidates for Masters of Writing posts; now every post was filled by special decree without the usual probation.

sentences.zh: 固乃與廷尉吳雄上疏，以為八使所糾，宜急誅罰，選舉署置，可歸有司。

translations.literal: Gu thereupon with Commandant of Justice Wu Xiong submitted a memorial holding that what the eight envoys had impeached ought to face swift execution or dismissal, while selections and appointments could be returned to the responsible offices.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu and Commandant Wu Xiong jointly urged that the inspectors’ targets be punished at once and that routine appointments be left again to the proper ministries.

sentences.zh: 帝感其言，乃更下免八使所舉刺史、二千石，自是稀復特拜，切責三公，明加考察，朝廷稱善。

translations.literal: The emperor was moved by their words and thereupon again issued orders dismissing the inspectors and two-thousand-piculs whom the eight envoys had recommended; from this time special appointments were rare, the Three Excellencies were sharply rebuked, investigation was clearly increased, and the court praised the reform.
translations.idiomatic: Moved by the memorial, the emperor removed the officials the envoys had denounced, curtailed ad hoc appointments, tightened oversight of the Three Excellencies, and won praise across the government.

sentences.zh: 乃復與光祿勳劉宣上言：「自頃選舉牧守，多非其人，至行無道，侵害百姓。

translations.literal: Thereupon again with Minister of the Imperial Household Liu Xuan he submitted: “Of late, in choosing shepherds and guardians, many have not been fit for their posts, to the point of behaving lawlessly and harming the common people.”
translations.idiomatic: With Liu Xuan, Minister of the Imperial Household, he added: “Recent nominations for regional governors have too often produced men unfit for office—men who trample the law and prey on the people.”

sentences.zh: 又宜止盤遊，專心庶政。

translations.literal: Moreover it is fitting to stop roaming excursions and concentrate the mind on the myriad tasks of government.
translations.idiomatic: He also urged the emperor to give up pleasure tours and fix his mind on the daily business of rule.

sentences.zh: 」帝納其言，於是下詔諸州劾奏守令以下，政有乖枉，遇人無惠者，免所居官；

translations.literal: ” The emperor accepted their words; thereupon he issued an edict to the provinces to impeach administrators and magistrates and below: where governance was perverse and wrong and they showed no kindness to the people, dismiss them from the posts they occupy;
translations.idiomatic: The throne agreed and ordered every province to impeach local officials who had governed unjustly or shown no mercy to the people;

sentences.zh: 其奸穢重罪，收付詔獄。

translations.literal: those whose wickedness and filth constituted heavy crimes, arrest and deliver them to the imperial prison.
translations.idiomatic: anyone guilty of serious corruption was to be jailed in the capital prison.

sentences.zh: 注[三]江京、劉安等坐省門下，孫程與王康等就斬京、安等，立順帝也。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: Jiang Jing, Liu An, and others sat below the palace gate; Sun Cheng with Wang Kang and others thereupon beheaded Jing, An, and others and enthroned Emperor Shun.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 3: the coup in which Sun Cheng executed Jiang Jing and Liu An at the gate and raised Emperor Shun.

sentences.zh: 固以清河王蒜年長有德，欲立之，謂梁冀曰：「今當立帝，宜擇長年高明有德，任親政事者，願將軍審詳大計，察周、霍之立文、宣，[一]戒鄧、閻之利幼弱。」

translations.literal: Gu held that the Prince of Qinghe, Suan, was advanced in years and possessed virtue, and wished to establish him; he said to Liang Ji: “Now when we shall establish an emperor, it is fitting to choose someone mature, enlightened, and virtuous, able personally to attend to government; I hope the general will deliberate the great plan carefully, observing how Zhou Bo enthroned Wen-di and Huo Guang enthroned Xuan-di, [note 1] and take warning from the Deng and Yan clans’ profit from enthroning the very young.”
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu favored Prince Suan of Qinghe, a grown man of known virtue, and told Liang Ji to follow the precedents of Zhou Bo and Huo Guang in picking an adult ruler, not repeat the mistake of letting child emperors serve the Deng and Yan families.

sentences.zh: [二]冀不從，乃立樂安王子纘，年八歲，是為質帝。

translations.literal: [Note 2] Ji did not follow this; thereupon he established the son of the Prince of Le’an, Zuan, aged eight sui—this was Emperor Zhi.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji refused, enthroned the eight-year-old son of the Prince of Le’an, Liu Zuan, who is known as Emperor Zhi.

sentences.zh: 時沖帝將北卜山陵，固乃議曰：「今處處寇賊，軍興用費加倍，新創憲陵，賦發非一。

translations.literal: At the time the late Emperor Shun was about to be buried northward on the mountain, Gu thereupon debated: “Now everywhere are bandits and rebels; military outlays have doubled; the new Xianling mausoleum has been founded and levies are not of one kind.”
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Shun’s funeral train was to go north to the tomb, Li Gu argued that banditry everywhere had doubled military costs and that the new imperial tomb had already drained many levies.

sentences.zh: 帝尚幼小，可起陵於憲陵塋內，依康陵制度，[三]其於役費三分減一。

translations.literal: The emperor is still young and small; it is possible to raise a tomb within the Xianling cemetery enclosure, following the Kangling regulations, [note 3] then for labor and expense reduce by one third.
translations.idiomatic: The child emperor could be buried inside Emperor Shun’s Xianling precinct on the model of Emperor Shang’s modest Kangling, cutting labor and expense by a third.

sentences.zh: 」乃從固議。

translations.literal: ” Thereupon he followed Gu’s proposal.
translations.idiomatic: The court accepted Li Gu’s plan.

sentences.zh: 時太后以比遭不造，委任宰輔，固所匡正，每輒從用，其黃門宦者一皆斥遣，天下鹹望遂平，而梁冀猜專，每相忌疾。

translations.literal: At that time the empress dowager, because she had repeatedly suffered ill fortune, entrusted affairs to the chief ministers; whatever Gu corrected and rectified she generally followed and employed; the Yellow Gate eunuchs were all dismissed and sent away—all under Heaven hoped order would be restored, yet Liang Ji was suspicious and domineering and constantly envied and resented him.
translations.idiomatic: The Dowager, worn by repeated bereavements, leaned on her ministers and usually took Li Gu’s advice, purging the palace eunuchs; the empire hoped for peace, but Liang Ji grew jealous of any check on his power.

sentences.zh: 注[一]周勃立文帝，霍光立宣帝也。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: Zhou Bo enthroned Wen-di; Huo Guang enthroned Xuan-di.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 1: Zhou Bo raised Emperor Wen; Huo Guang raised Emperor Xuan.

sentences.zh: 注[二]謂鄧太后立殤帝，帝時誕育百餘日，二歲而崩；

translations.literal: Comment [2]: it refers to Empress Dowager Deng’s enthroning Emperor Shang—the emperor at birth was a bit more than a hundred days and died at two sui;
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 2: the Deng regents once enthroned the infant Emperor Shang, who died at two;

sentences.zh: 又立安帝，時年十餘歲。

translations.literal: again she enthroned Emperor An, who at the time was a bit more than ten sui.
translations.idiomatic: they then set up Emperor An, then a boy of about ten.

sentences.zh: 閻太后立北鄉侯，其年薨，又征諸王子，擬擇立之也。

translations.literal: Empress Dowager Yan enthroned the Marquis of Beixiang; that same year he died, and again she summoned the imperial princes, planning to choose and establish one.
translations.idiomatic: Empress Dowager Yan tried the Marquis of Beixiang, who died the same year, then cast about among the princes for another puppet.

sentences.zh: 注[三]康陵，殤帝陵也。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: Kangling is Emperor Shang’s tomb.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 3: Kangling is the mausoleum of the short-lived Emperor Shang.

sentences.zh: 初，順帝時諸所除官，多不以次，及固在事，奏免百餘人。

translations.literal: At the beginning, in Emperor Shun’s time the offices appointed were often not in proper order; when Gu held office he memorialized to dismiss more than a hundred men.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Shun many offices had been sold or misfilled; once in power Li Gu secured the dismissal of over a hundred appointees.

sentences.zh: 此等既怨，又希望冀旨，遂共作飛章虛誣固罪曰：「臣聞君不稽古，無以承天；

translations.literal: These men both resented him and also hoped for Ji’s intent; thereupon together they fabricated a flying memorial falsely accusing Gu of crimes, stating: “Your servant has heard that if the lord does not examine antiquity, he has no means to receive Heaven;
translations.idiomatic: Those ousted officials nursed a grudge and curried Liang Ji’s favor by circulating an anonymous memorial: “A ruler who ignores antiquity cannot answer Heaven’s charge;

sentences.zh: [一]臣不述舊，無以奉君。

translations.literal: [Note 1] if the minister does not transmit the old, he has no means to serve the lord.”
translations.idiomatic: and a minister who forgets precedent cannot serve his sovereign.”

sentences.zh: 昔堯殂之後，舜仰慕三年，坐則見堯於牆，食則鶯堯於羹。

translations.literal: After Yao’s death, Shun mourned and yearned for three years; sitting, he saw Yao on the wall; eating, he saw Yao in his broth.
translations.idiomatic: After Yao died, Shun mourned three years until he seemed to see Yao in every wall and taste him in every bowl.

sentences.zh: [二]斯所謂聿追來孝，不失臣子之節者。

translations.literal: [Note 2] this is what is called earnestly pursuing the filial piety to come, not losing the demeanor of minister and son.
translations.idiomatic: That is the “dutiful son who follows his father’s way” praised in the Odes—true loyalty between ruler and subject.

sentences.zh: [三]太尉李固，因公假私，依正行邪，離閒近戚，自隆支黨。

translations.literal: [Note 3] Grand Commandant Li Gu, using public office to borrow private ends, relying on the straight to practice the crooked, estranging the near kin, himself exalting his factional followers.
translations.idiomatic: They accused Grand Commandant Li Gu of twisting public office for private ends, slandering the imperial in-laws, and building a clique.

sentences.zh: 至於表舉薦達，例皆門徒；

translations.literal: As for memorializing recommendations and promotions, in every case they were his disciples;
translations.idiomatic: Every man he recommended to high office, they said, was his protégé;

sentences.zh: 及所辟召，靡非先舊。

translations.literal: and those whom he summoned to appointment were without exception former associates.
translations.idiomatic: and everyone he called to office was an old ally.

sentences.zh: 或富室財賂，或子豻婚屬，其列在官牒者凡四十九人。

translations.literal: Some were rich houses’ bribes, some were sons-in-law and marriage kin; those listed on the official registers in all number forty-nine men.
translations.idiomatic: Forty-nine names on the rolls, they charged, were bought with gold or tied by marriage.

sentences.zh: 又廣選賈豎，以補令史；

translations.literal: Moreover he broadly selected merchants and peddlers to fill clerkships;
translations.idiomatic: He supposedly packed the clerkships with shopkeepers;

sentences.zh: 募求好馬，臨□呈試。

translations.literal: he recruited and sought fine horses, facing [damaged] to present them for trial.
translations.idiomatic: and staged horse trials [one character illegible] to show off costly mounts.

sentences.zh: 出入踰侈，輜軿曜日。

translations.literal: Going out and entering he exceeded extravagance; covered carriages dazzled the sun.
translations.idiomatic: His retinue and curtained carriages flaunted wealth in broad daylight.

sentences.zh: 大行在殯，路人掩涕，固獨胡粉飾貌，搔頭弄姿，[四]盤旋偃仰，從容冶步，曾無慘怛傷悴之心。

translations.literal: The late emperor lay in the coffin while travelers on the road covered their tears—only Gu alone powdered his face with lead, scratching his head and posturing, [note 4] wheeling and reclining, pacing with studied allure, utterly without a heart grieved and haggard with sorrow.
translations.idiomatic: While the catafalque still stood in the palace, passersby wept—yet Li Gu, they claimed, powdered his face and minced about the court as if at a levee, showing no grief.

sentences.zh: 山陵未成，違矯舊政，善則稱己，過則歸君，斥逐近臣，不得侍送，作威作福，莫固之甚。

translations.literal: Before the mountain tomb was completed he wantonly altered old policies: good deeds he ascribed to himself, faults he returned to the lord; he drove away the near attendants so they could not attend the funeral; he made awe and made blessing—none seized power more ruthlessly than Gu.
translations.idiomatic: Before the tomb was finished he overturned precedent, claimed every success and blamed the throne for every failure, barred palace attendants from the obsequies, and wielded ministerial authority as no one had before.

sentences.zh: 臣聞台輔之位，實和陰陽，琁機不平，寇賊奸軌，[五]則責在太尉。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that the place of the terrace ministers truly harmonizes yin and yang; when the jade pivots are not level, bandits, rebels, traitors, and inner villains [note 5]—then the responsibility lies with the Grand Commandant.
translations.idiomatic: The Three Excellencies harmonize Heaven and Earth; when the cosmic pivots slip and bandits multiply, the Grand Commandant bears the blame.

sentences.zh: [六]固受任之後，東南跋扈，兩州數郡，[七]千里蕭條，兆人傷損，大化陵□，而詆疵先主，苟肆狂狷。

translations.literal: [Note 6] After Gu received appointment, the southeast ran rampant; two provinces and several commanderies, [note 7] a thousand li desolate, the myriad people injured and harmed, great transformation [damaged], yet he slandered the former lord and wantonly indulged wild perversity.
translations.idiomatic: Since his appointment the southeast has burned while thousands of li lie waste—yet he reviles the late emperor and struts unchecked [one character damaged in “great transformation”].

sentences.zh: 存無廷爭之忠，沒有誹謗之說。

translations.literal: Alive he had no loyalty of disputing at court; dead there would be slanderous talk.
translations.idiomatic: In life he never spoke truth at audience; in death he would leave only malice.

sentences.zh: 夫子罪莫大於累父，臣惡莫深於毀君。

translations.literal: Of the master’s crimes none is greater than burdening the father; of the minister’s wickedness none is deeper than destroying the lord.
translations.idiomatic: No son’s sin is heavier than shaming his father; no minister’s crime is blacker than traducing his ruler.

sentences.zh: 固之過釁，事合誅辟。

translations.literal: Gu’s faults and crimes, in principle call for execution.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu’s offenses deserve death.

sentences.zh: 」[八]事奏，冀以白太后，使下其事。
