translations.literal: ” [Note 8] when the memorial was submitted, Ji reported it to the empress dowager and had her order the matter sent down.
translations.idiomatic: ” When the document reached Liang Ji, he showed it to the Dowager and asked her to order an inquiry.

sentences.zh: 太后不聽，得免。

translations.literal: The empress dowager did not listen; he obtained pardon.
translations.idiomatic: She refused to act, and Li Gu escaped conviction.

sentences.zh: 注[一]書曰：「粵若稽古帝堯。

translations.literal: Comment [1] the Documents state: “Yue ruo ji gu Di Yao.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 1: the Shang shu opens, “Examining antiquity, Emperor Yao…”

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄注曰：「稽，同也。

translations.literal: ” Zheng Xuan’s commentary states: “Ji means same.”
translations.idiomatic: ” Zheng Xuan glosses ji as “to match” or “align with.”

sentences.zh: 古，天也。

translations.literal: Gu means Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Gu here means “Heaven” or “high antiquity.”

sentences.zh: 言能同天而行者帝堯。」

translations.literal: It means one who can align with Heaven in conduct is Emperor Yao.”
translations.idiomatic: The line praises a ruler whose conduct matches Heaven—here, Emperor Yao.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]太公兵法曰：「帝堯王天下之時，金銀珠玉弗服也，錦銹文綺弗衣也，奇怪異物弗視也，玩好之器弗寶也，淫佚之樂弗聽也，宮垣室屋弗堊色也，榱桷柱楹弗藻飾也，茅茨之蓋弗翦齊也，滋味重累弗食也，溫飯暖羹酸餧不易也。」

translations.literal: Comment [2] the Taigong’s Military Treatise states: “When Emperor Yao ruled all under Heaven, he did not wear gold, silver, pearls, or jade; he did not clothe himself in brocade embroidery with patterns; he did not look at strange and marvelous things; he did not treasure instruments of play and fondness; he did not listen to dissolute and excessive music; he did not smear colors on palace walls and chambers; he did not paint and ornament rafters, ridgepoles, pillars, or lintels; he did not trim even the thatch roof evenly; he did not eat flavors piled in excess; he did not change warm rice, mild soup, sour, or spoiled food.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 2: the apocryphal military canon ascribes to Yao a life of deliberate austerity—plain clothes, plain food, undecorated halls.

sentences.zh: 注[三]聿，述也。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: yu means transmit.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 3: yu means “to carry on” or “hand down.”

sentences.zh: 詩大雅曰：「文王烝哉，遹追來孝。

translations.literal: The Greater Odes of the Classic of Poetry state: “King Wen is radiant; earnestly he pursues the filial piety to come.”
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Poetry says of King Wen that he “carried forward the filial duty that reaches his forebears.”

sentences.zh: 」言文王能述追王季勤孝之行也。

translations.literal: ” It means King Wen was able to transmit and pursue King Ji’s diligent filial conduct.
translations.idiomatic: ” That is, King Wen continued King Ji’s devoted service to his father.

sentences.zh: 注[四]西京雜記曰：「武帝遇李夫人，就取玉簪搔頭，自此宮人搔頭皆用玉。」

translations.literal: Comment [4] the Miscellaneous Records of the Western Capital states: “When Emperor Wu met Lady Li, he took her jade hairpin to scratch his head; from this palace women in scratching their heads all used jade.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 4: Liu Xiang’s miscellany says Emperor Wu borrowed Lady Li’s jade hairpin to scratch his head, starting a court fashion.

sentences.zh: 注[五]書曰：「琁機玉衡以齊七政。

translations.literal: Comment [5] the Documents state: “He set in motion the jade pivots and jade transverse tube to align the seven regulators.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 5: the Shang shu describes the celestial instrument used to align the seven luminaries.

sentences.zh: 」孔安國注曰：「琁，美玉也。

translations.literal: ” Kong Anguo’s commentary states: “Xuan is fine jade.”
translations.idiomatic: ” Kong Anguo glosses the instrument’s jade components.

sentences.zh: 機，衡也。

translations.literal: Ji means transverse tube.
translations.idiomatic: Ji is the crossbar of the sighting instrument.

sentences.zh: 王者正天文之器，可運轉者也。

translations.literal: It is the king’s instrument for correcting astronomy; it can revolve and turn.
translations.idiomatic: It is the sovereign’s armilary sphere, meant to be turned and read.

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「寇賊奸軌。

translations.literal: ” It also states: “Bandits, rebels, traitors, and inner villains.”
translations.idiomatic: ” Another passage lists “bandits, rebels, traitors, and villains within.”

sentences.zh: 」注曰：「腢行攻劫曰寇，殺人曰賊，在外曰奸，在內曰軌。」

translations.literal: ” The commentary states: “Those who in bands conduct raids are called bandits; those who kill people are called rebels; those outside are called traitors; those inside are called villains.”
translations.idiomatic: ” The gloss distinguishes bandits who raid in packs, murderers called “rebels,” outside agitators, and inside conspirators.”

sentences.zh: 注[六]續漢志曰「太尉掌四方兵事功課，歲盡則奏殿最而行賞罰」也。

translations.literal: Comment [6] the Continuation of the Han Monograph states: “The Grand Commandant manages military affairs and merit evaluation in the four directions; when the year ends he memorializes best and worst and carries out rewards and punishments.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 6: the Later Han offices treatise makes the Grand Commandant answer for military security and year-end performance reviews.

sentences.zh: 注[七]謂九江賊徐鳳、馬免等攻燒城邑，廣陵賊張嬰等攻殺江都長。

translations.literal: Comment [7]: it refers to the Jiujiang bandits Xu Feng and Ma Mian and others attacking and burning cities and towns, and the Guangling bandits Zhang Ying and others attacking and killing the magistrate of Jiangdu.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 7: the outbreaks led by Xu Feng and Ma Mian in Jiujiang and Zhang Ying in Guangling.

sentences.zh: 九江、廣陵是荊、楊之地，故雲兩州也。

translations.literal: Jiujiang and Guangling are in Jing and Yang regions; therefore it says two provinces.
translations.idiomatic: Those commanderies lay in Jingzhou and Yangzhou—hence “two provinces.”

sentences.zh: 注[八]據吳佑傳，此章馬融之詞。

translations.literal: Comment [8]: according to Wu You’s biography, this memorial’s language is Ma Rong’s.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 8: Wu You’s biography attributes the forged text to Ma Rong’s pen.

sentences.zh: 冀忌帝聰慧，恐為後患，遂令左右進鴆。

translations.literal: Ji feared the emperor’s cleverness and feared he would become a later trouble; thereupon he ordered those at his side to present poisoned ale.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji feared the boy’s intelligence and ordered an attendant to bring poisoned gruel.

sentences.zh: 帝苦煩甚，使促召固。

translations.literal: The emperor suffered vexing distress extremely and ordered them urgently to summon Gu.
translations.idiomatic: The child grew violently ill and cried for Li Gu to be fetched.

sentences.zh: 固入，前問：「陛下得患所由？

translations.literal: Gu entered and stepped forward asking: “Your Majesty, from what did you obtain this illness?”
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu rushed in and asked what had brought on the sudden illness.

sentences.zh: 」帝尚能言，曰：「食□餅，今腹中悶，得水尚可活。

translations.literal: ” The emperor was still able to speak and said: “I ate [damaged] cakes; now in the belly it is stifling; obtaining water I could still live.”
translations.idiomatic: ” The emperor could still whisper that he had eaten a cake, that his belly was knotted with pain, and that water might save him.

sentences.zh: 」時冀亦在側，曰：「恐吐，不可飲水。

translations.literal: ” At that time Ji also was at his side and said: “I fear vomiting; he cannot drink water.”
translations.idiomatic: ” Liang Ji, standing beside the bed, said water would only make him vomit.

sentences.zh: 」語未絕而崩。

translations.literal: ” Before the words were finished he died.
translations.idiomatic: ” Before the sentence ended the boy was dead.

sentences.zh: 固伏屍號哭，推舉侍醫。

translations.literal: Gu lay on the corpse wailing; he pushed and raised the attending physician.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu threw himself on the body, weeping, and seized the court physician for questioning.

sentences.zh: 冀慮其事洩，大惡之。

translations.literal: Ji worried that the affair would leak and greatly hated him.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji feared exposure and hated Li Gu all the more.

sentences.zh: 因議立嗣，固引司徒胡廣、司空趙戒，[一]先與冀書曰：「天下不幸，仍遭大憂。

translations.literal: When they deliberated on establishing the heir, Gu cited Minister of Education Hu Guang and Minister of Works Zhao Jie, [note 1] and first wrote to Ji: “Under Heaven unhappily again suffers great mourning.”
translations.idiomatic: In the debate over the succession Li Gu enlisted Hu Guang and Zhao Jie and wrote first to Liang Ji: “The empire mourns another untimely death.”

sentences.zh: 皇太后聖德當朝，攝統萬機，明將軍體履忠孝，憂存社稷，而頻年之閒，國祚三絕。

translations.literal: The empress dowager’s sagely virtue presides over the court; she holds the myriad threads; the brilliant general in person practices loyalty and filial piety and stores care for the altars of soil and grain—yet within a few years the national fate has thrice been cut off.
translations.idiomatic: The Dowager’s virtue steadies the court, and you, General, claim to guard the altars—yet three emperors have died in quick succession.

sentences.zh: [二]今當立帝，天下重器，誠知太后垂心，將軍勞慮，詳擇其人，務存聖明。

translations.literal: [Note 2] Now when we shall establish an emperor, it is the heavy vessel of all under Heaven; truly we know the empress dowager hangs her heart and the general toils in thought, choosing the person in detail, striving to preserve sagely enlightenment.
translations.idiomatic: Choosing the next ruler is the heaviest charge in the realm; we know you and the Dowager weigh every candidate in search of a worthy man.

sentences.zh: 然愚情眷眷，竊獨有懷。

translations.literal: Yet your servant’s foolish feeling clings anxiously; alone he harbors a thought.
translations.idiomatic: Yet I cannot help adding one plea of my own.

sentences.zh: 遠尋先世廢立舊儀，近見國家踐祚前事，未嘗不詢訪公卿，廣求腢議，令上應天心，下合觿望。

translations.literal: Searching far in former ages’ old rites for deposing and enthroning, examining near the precedents before our state’s ascending the throne, there has never been a case of not consulting the excellencies and ministers and broadly seeking the throng’s counsel, ordering above to answer Heaven’s mind and below to match the multitude’s expectation.
translations.idiomatic: Every past enthronement consulted the high ministers and listened to public opinion so that Heaven and the people might both be satisfied.

sentences.zh: 且永初以來，政事多謬，地震宮廟，彗星竟天，誠是將軍用情之日。

translations.literal: Moreover since the Yongchu era government affairs have mostly been in error; earthquakes struck palace temples; a comet filled the sky—truly this is the day for the general to exert sincere feeling.
translations.idiomatic: Since the Yongchu reign omens have multiplied—tremors in the palace, a comet across the sky—this is the moment for deepest sincerity.

sentences.zh: 傳曰：『以天下與人易，為天下得人難。

translations.literal: The tradition states: “To yield the empire to another is easy; to win the right man for the empire is hard.”
translations.idiomatic: The classics say, “Giving away the realm is easy; finding a worthy to receive it is hard.”

sentences.zh: 』昔昌邑之立，昏亂日滋，霍光憂愧發憤，悔之折骨。

translations.literal: ” Formerly at the enthronement of King Changyi, darkness and disorder daily increased; Huo Guang, worried and ashamed, roused himself in anger, regretting it to the breaking of bones.
translations.idiomatic: ” King Changyi’s brief reign showed how quickly a bad choice brings chaos—Huo Guang nearly broke himself with remorse.

sentences.zh: [三]自非博陸忠勇，[四]延年奮發，大漢之祀，幾將傾矣。

translations.literal: [Note 3] Had it not been for Bolu’s loyalty and courage, [note 4] Tian Yannian’s rousing effort, the sacrifices to Great Han would almost have toppled.
translations.idiomatic: Had not Huo Guang (Lord Bolu) and Tian Yannian acted with desperate loyalty, the Han house would have fallen then.

sentences.zh: [五]至憂至重，可不熟慮！

translations.literal: [Note 5] the utmost worry, the utmost weight—can you not deliberate maturely!
translations.idiomatic: The burden could not be heavier—think long before you choose.

sentences.zh: 悠悠萬事，唯此為大。

translations.literal: Of the myriad matters that drift along, only this is greatest.
translations.idiomatic: Among the endless affairs of state, none outweighs this.

sentences.zh: 國之興衰，在此一舉。

translations.literal: The state’s rise and fall hang on this one act.
translations.idiomatic: The dynasty’s fate turns on this single decision.

sentences.zh: 」冀得書，乃召三公、中二千石、列侯大議所立。

translations.literal: ” When Ji received the letter he then summoned the Three Excellencies, full two-thousand-piculs, and ranked nobles to debate greatly whom to establish.
translations.idiomatic: ” Liang Ji read the letter and convened the high nobility to debate the succession.

sentences.zh: 固、廣、戒及大鴻臚杜喬皆以為清河王蒜明德著聞，又屬最尊親，宜立為嗣。

translations.literal: Gu, Guang, Jie, and Grand Herald Du Qiao all held that the Prince of Qinghe, Suan, whose bright virtue was renowned in report, moreover belonged to the most honored kin, ought to be established as heir.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu, Hu Guang, Zhao Jie, and Grand Herald Du Qiao all named Prince Suan of Qinghe, the most senior and virtuous of the Liu princes.

sentences.zh: 先是蠡吾侯志當取冀妹，時在京師，冀欲立之。

translations.literal: Earlier the Marquis of Liwu, Zhi, was to take Ji’s younger sister as wife; at that time he was in the capital; Ji wished to establish him.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji preferred his future brother-in-law Zhi, Marquis of Liwu, who was already in Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 觿論既異，憤憤不得意，而未有以相奪。

translations.literal: The throng’s discussions being already divergent, they were angry and displeased yet had no means to seize the decision from one another.
translations.idiomatic: Opinion was split; neither side could yet force a decision.

sentences.zh: [六]中常侍曹騰等聞而夜往說冀曰：「將軍累世有椒房之親，秉攝萬機，賓客縱橫，多有過差。

translations.literal: [Note 6] Regular Palace Attendant Cao Teng and others heard and in the night went to persuade Ji, saying: “The general’s house for generations has had pepper-chamber kinship; you hold and grasp the myriad threads; guests and clients run rampant and many have faults and excesses.
translations.idiomatic: That night Regular Attendant Cao Teng warned Liang Ji: “Your clan has married into the palace for generations; your clients break every rule;

sentences.zh: 清河王嚴明，若果立，則將軍受禍不久矣。

translations.literal: The Prince of Qinghe is strict and bright; if indeed he is established, then the general will receive disaster before long.”
translations.idiomatic: Prince Suan is strict and perceptive—if he mounts the throne your house will not survive long.”

sentences.zh: 不如立蠡吾侯，富貴可長保也。

translations.literal: It is not as good as establishing the Marquis of Liwu—wealth and honor can be long preserved.”
translations.idiomatic: Better enthrone the Marquis of Liwu and keep your fortune secure.”

sentences.zh: 」冀然其言。

translations.literal: ” Ji considered his words right.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji took the advice.

sentences.zh: 明日重會公卿，冀意氣凶凶，而言辭激切。

translations.literal: The next day he again convened the excellencies and ministers; Ji’s manner and qi were fierce and his words sharp and cutting.
translations.idiomatic: Next day he reconvened the assembly, truculent and sharp-tongued.

sentences.zh: 自胡廣、趙戒以下，莫不懾憚之。

translations.literal: From Hu Guang and Zhao Jie downward, none did not fear him.
translations.idiomatic: From Hu Guang and Zhao Jie downward, no one dared defy him.

sentences.zh: 皆曰：

translations.literal: All said:
translations.idiomatic: They chorused:

sentences.zh: 「惟大將軍令。

translations.literal: “We await only the Grand General’s command.”
translations.idiomatic: “We await the Grand General’s order alone.”

sentences.zh: 」而固獨與杜喬堅守本議。

translations.literal: ” Yet Gu alone with Du Qiao firmly maintained the original proposal.
translations.idiomatic: Only Li Gu and Du Qiao held out for Prince Suan.

sentences.zh: 冀厲聲曰：「罷會。

translations.literal: Ji shouted in a harsh voice: “Dismiss the meeting.”
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji snarled, “This session is ended.”

sentences.zh: 」固意既不從，猶望觿心可立，復以書勸冀。

translations.literal: ” Gu’s intent having not been followed, he still hoped the multitude’s hearts could support the enthronement; again with a letter he urged Ji.
translations.idiomatic: Though outvoted, Li Gu still hoped public sentiment would favor Prince Suan and wrote again to Liang Ji.

sentences.zh: 冀愈激怒，乃說太后先策免固，竟立蠡吾侯，是為桓帝。

translations.literal: Ji grew all the more enraged; thereupon he persuaded the empress dowager first to order Gu dismissed by edict, and in the end established the Marquis of Liwu—this was Emperor Huan.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji, furious, persuaded the Dowager to strip Li Gu of office first, then enthroned the Marquis of Liwu as Emperor Huan.

sentences.zh: 注[一]謝承書「戒字志伯，蜀郡成都人也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Xie Cheng’s book: “Jie’s character is Zhibo; he was a man of Chengdu in Shu commandery.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 1: Xie Cheng gives Zhao Jie’s style as Zhibo and his origin as Chengdu.

sentences.zh: 戒博學明經講授，舉孝廉，累遷荊州刺史。

translations.literal: Jie was broadly learned, clarified the classics in lecturing, was recommended as filial and incorrupt, and by stages was promoted to Inspector of Jing Province.
translations.idiomatic: He rose through learning and the classics, took the “filial and incorrupt” path, and eventually became inspector of Jing Province.

sentences.zh: 梁商弟讓為南陽太守，恃椒房之寵，不奉法，戒到州，劾奏之。

translations.literal: Liang Shang’s younger brother Rang was Administrator of Nanyang; relying on pepper-chamber favor he did not obey the law; when Jie arrived in the province he impeached him.
translations.idiomatic: When Liang Rang, brother of Liang Shang, abused his post as governor of Nanyang, Zhao Jie impeached him on arrival.

sentences.zh: 遷戒河閒相。

translations.literal: Jie was transferred to be chancellor of Hejian.
translations.idiomatic: He was then named chancellor of the kingdom of Hejian.

sentences.zh: 以冀部難理，整厲威嚴。

translations.literal: Because Ji’s territory was hard to govern, he tightened and inspired awe and severity.
translations.idiomatic: Hejian was unruly, so he ruled with strict authority.

sentences.zh: 遷南陽太守。

translations.literal: He was promoted to Administrator of Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: He returned as governor of Nanyang.

sentences.zh: 糾豪傑，恤吏人，奏免中官貴戚子弟為令長貪濁者。

translations.literal: He corrected the powerful and heroic, showed pity to clerks and people, and memorialized to dismiss eunuchs’ and magnates’ sons who as magistrates were greedy and turbid.
translations.idiomatic: He curbed local strongmen, looked after officials and commoners, and removed corrupt magistrates who were kin of eunuchs and great clans.

sentences.zh: 征拜為尚書令，出為河南尹，轉拜太常。

translations.literal: He was summoned and appointed Director of the Secretariat, went out as Intendant of Henan, and was transferred and appointed Minister of Ceremonies.
translations.idiomatic: He served as Director of the Secretariat, Intendant of Henan, and Minister of Ceremonies in turn.

sentences.zh: 永和六年特拜司空」也。

translations.literal: In the sixth year of Yonghe he received special appointment as Minister of Works.”
translations.idiomatic: In Yonghe 6 he was specially named Minister of Works.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]順帝崩，沖帝立一年崩，質帝一年崩。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: Emperor Shun died; Emperor Chong was established one year and died; Emperor Zhi one year and died.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 2: Emperor Shun died, Emperor Chong reigned one year and died, Emperor Zhi one year and died.

sentences.zh: 注[三]昌邑王賀，武帝孫昌邑哀王子也。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: King He of Changyi was Emperor Wu’s grandson, son of King Ai of Changyi.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 3: the deposed King He of Changyi was a grandson of Emperor Wu.

sentences.zh: 昭帝崩，霍光立之。

translations.literal: When Emperor Zhao died, Huo Guang established him.
translations.idiomatic: On Emperor Zhao’s death Huo Guang raised him to the throne.

sentences.zh: 注[四]霍光封博陸侯。

translations.literal: Comment [4]: Huo Guang was enfeoffed as Marquis of Bolu.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 4: Huo Guang held the title Marquis of Bolu.

sentences.zh: 前書音義曰：「博，大。

translations.literal: The Former Han Documents sounds and meanings state: “Bo means great.”
translations.idiomatic: An earlier commentary glosses bo as “great.”

sentences.zh: 陸，平。

translations.literal: Lu means level.
translations.idiomatic: Lu means “level” or “peaceful.”

sentences.zh: 取其嘉名，無此縣也。

translations.literal: Taking its auspicious name, there is no such county.
translations.idiomatic: The title was an honorific bundle of graphs, not the name of a single county.

sentences.zh: 食邑北海、河東也。」

translations.literal: His food fiefs were in Beihai and Hedong.”
translations.idiomatic: His income came from estates in Beihai and Hedong.”

sentences.zh: 注[五]霍光召丞相已下議曰：「昌邑王行昏亂，恐危社稷，如何？

translations.literal: Comment [5] Huo Guang summoned the chancellor and below to deliberate, saying: “The King of Changyi’s conduct is dark and chaotic; I fear he endangers the altars of soil and grain—what is to be done?”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 5: Huo Guang asked his colleagues whether King He’s debauchery threatened the state.

sentences.zh: 」腢臣皆驚愕失色。

translations.literal: ” The throng of ministers were stunned and lost color.
translations.idiomatic: ” The ministers sat in shocked silence.

sentences.zh: 大司農田延年前離席案□曰：「今日之議，不得旋踵，腢臣後應者，臣請□斬之！

translations.literal: Grand Minister of Agriculture Tian Yannian left his seat, pressed [damaged] the table, and said: “Today’s deliberation may not turn on its heel; any minister who responds late—I request [damaged] to behead him!”
translations.idiomatic: Tian Yannien leaped up, struck the table, and swore that anyone who hesitated would die under the axe [characters damaged].

sentences.zh: 」於是廢立遂定。

translations.literal: ” Thereupon the deposition and enthronement was settled.
translations.idiomatic: ” On that word King He was deposed.

sentences.zh: 注[六]未有別理而易奪之。

translations.literal: Comment [6]: there was no separate reason yet they easily removed him.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 6: Li Gu had given no fresh cause for dismissal yet was stripped at will.

sentences.zh: 後歲餘，甘陵劉文、魏郡劉鮪各謀立蒜為天子，梁冀因此誣固與文、鮪共為妖言，下獄。

translations.literal: After more than a year, Liu Wen of Ganling and Liu Wei of Wei commandery each plotted to establish Suan as Son of Heaven; Liang Ji thereupon falsely accused Gu of together with Wen and Wei making prodigious speech, and cast him into prison.
translations.idiomatic: A year later Liu Wen and Liu Wei raised Prince Suan’s banner; Liang Ji framed Li Gu as their accomplice and jailed him.

sentences.zh: 門生勃海王調貫械上書，證固之枉，河內趙承等數十人亦要鈇鍎詣闕通訴，[一]太后明之，乃赦焉。

translations.literal: His disciple Wang Tiao of Bohai, wearing cangue, submitted a memorial proving Gu’s injustice; several tens of men such as Zhao Cheng of Henei also demanded axes and went to the palace gate to lodge complaint, [note 1] the empress dowager understood and thereupon pardoned him.
translations.idiomatic: His student Wang Tiao wore the convict’s collar to petition for justice, and dozens led by Zhao Cheng of Henei gathered at the gate with executioners’ axes until the Dowager saw the truth and ordered his release.

sentences.zh: 及出獄，京師市裡皆稱萬歲。

translations.literal: When he came out of prison, the capital’s wards and lanes all cried “ten thousand years.”
translations.idiomatic: When Li Gu walked free the capital streets rang with cheers.

sentences.zh: 冀聞之大驚，畏固名德終為己害，乃更據奏前事，遂誅之，時年五十四。

translations.literal: When Ji heard this he was greatly alarmed; he feared Gu’s fame and virtue would in the end become a harm to himself; thereupon he again seized and memorialized the former affair, and thereupon executed him; at the time his age was fifty-four.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji, terrified by Li Gu’s popularity, re-opened the old charges and had him killed at fifty-four.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]字林曰：「鈇鍎，椹也。

translations.literal: [Note 2] Comment [1] the Zilin states: “Fu-zhi is the executioner’s block.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: the word list defines the heavy axe and block carried to the gate.

sentences.zh: 」鍎音質。

translations.literal: ” Zhi is read like zhi.
translations.idiomatic: Zhi is glossed with the “quality” reading.

sentences.zh: 椹音竹心反。

translations.literal: Zhen is read with the fanqie bamboo-heart.
translations.idiomatic: Zhen uses the fanqie spelling “bamboo + heart.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]固臨終，□子孫素棺三寸，幅巾，殯殮於本郡磽埆之地，不得還墓塋，污先公兆域。

translations.literal: Comment [2] when Gu faced death he [damaged] his sons and grandsons a plain coffin three inches thick, head-wrap of plain cloth, and burial rites in his home commandery on stony barren ground, not being able to return to the tomb enclosure, defiling the former dukes’ grave precinct.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 2: Li Gu’s deathbed instructions—a thin pine coffin, plain winding sheet, burial in poor soil of his commandery lest his corpse pollute the family cemetery—are recorded in Xie Cheng.

sentences.zh: 見謝承書也。

translations.literal: It is seen in Xie Cheng’s book.
translations.idiomatic: The same details appear in Xie Cheng’s history.

sentences.zh: 臨命，與胡廣、趙戒書曰：「固受國厚恩，是以竭其股肱，不顧死亡，志欲扶持王室，比隆文、宣。

translations.literal: Facing death he wrote to Hu Guang and Zhao Jie: “Gu has received the state’s thick favor; therefore he exhausted his thigh and arm, not looking to death or life, his intent set on supporting the royal house and matching the elevation of Wen and Xuan.”
translations.idiomatic: Facing execution he wrote to Hu Guang and Zhao Jie: “The Han has loaded me with favors; I gave my limbs and life to raise it as Wen and Xuan were raised.”

sentences.zh: [一]何圖一朝梁氏迷謬，公等曲從，以吉為凶，成事為敗乎？

translations.literal: [Note 1] How could I have expected that in one morning the Liang house would go astray, and you gentlemen would bend to follow, taking the auspicious for the inauspicious, making the completed affair a defeat?”
translations.idiomatic: “Who could have dreamed that the Liang clan would lead you astray in one morning—that you would call good evil and turn victory into ruin?”

sentences.zh: 漢家衰微，從此始矣。

translations.literal: The house of Han’s decline and weakness begins from this.
translations.idiomatic: From this day the Han begins its slide.

sentences.zh: 公等受主厚祿，顛而不扶，傾覆大事，後之良史，豈有所私？

translations.literal: You gentlemen receive the ruler’s thick salaries yet when he topples you do not support him; when the great affair overturns, will later good historians show any partiality?
translations.idiomatic: You feed richly from the throne yet let it fall—do you think later historians will spare you?”

sentences.zh: 固身已矣，於義得矣，夫復何言！

translations.literal: Gu’s person is finished; as for right, he has obtained it—what more is there to say!
translations.idiomatic: For myself I am done; I have kept faith—there is nothing left to say.”

sentences.zh: 」廣、戒得書悲籩，皆長歎流涕。

translations.literal: ” When Guang and Jie received the letter they were grieved to the edge of weeping; both long sighed and shed tears.
translations.idiomatic: Hu Guang and Zhao Jie read the letter and wept aloud.

sentences.zh: 注[一]文帝、宣帝皆腢臣迎立，能興漢祚。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: Wen-di and Xuan-di were both raised by ministers and could revive Han’s fortune.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 1: Emperors Wen and Xuan were both brought to the throne by ministers and restored the dynasty.

sentences.zh: 州郡收固二子基、茲於郾城，皆死獄中。

translations.literal: The provinces and commanderies arrested Gu’s two sons Ji and Ci at Yancheng; both died in prison.
translations.idiomatic: Provincial agents seized his sons Li Ji and Li Ci at Yancheng and killed them in jail.

sentences.zh: [一]小子燮得脫亡命。

translations.literal: [Note 1] The youngest son Xie escaped and fled his life.
translations.idiomatic: His youngest son Li Xie alone escaped into hiding.

sentences.zh: 冀乃封廣、戒而露固屍於四衢，[二]令有敢臨者加其罪。

translations.literal: Ji thereupon enfeoffed Guang and Jie and exposed Gu’s corpse at the four crossroads, [note 2] ordering that whoever dared to mourn nearby would have guilt added.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji rewarded Hu Guang and Zhao Jie while displaying Li Gu’s body at a crossroads and threatening anyone who approached.

sentences.zh: 固弟子汝南郭亮，[三]年始成童，[四] 遊學洛陽，乃左提章鉞，[五]右秉鈇鍎，詣闕上書，乞收固屍。

translations.literal: Gu’s disciple Guo Liang of Runan, [note 3] his years just reaching “completed boy,” [note 4] studying abroad in Luoyang, then in his left hand carried the memorial and axe, [note 5] in his right grasped the fu-zhi, went to the palace gate and submitted a memorial begging to receive Gu’s corpse.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Liang of Runan, Li Gu’s pupil barely fifteen, came to the capital with petition and axe in hand to beg for his teacher’s body.

sentences.zh: 不許，因往臨哭，陳辭於前，遂守喪不去。

translations.literal: When not permitted, he thereupon went to mourn weeping, set forth words before it, and thereupon guarded the mourning and did not leave.
translations.idiomatic: Refused, he wept at the corpse and refused to leave.

sentences.zh: 夏門亭長呵之曰：[六]「李、杜二公為大臣，不能安上納忠，而興造無端。

translations.literal: The Xia Gate precinct chief shouted at him: [note 6] “The two dukes Li and Du were great ministers yet could not pacify the ruler and accept loyalty, and instead stirred up baseless trouble.”
translations.idiomatic: The warden of the Xia Gate barracks snarled: “Li Gu and Du Qiao were chief ministers who could not steady the throne yet stirred endless trouble.”

sentences.zh: 卿曹何等腐生，公犯詔書，干試有司乎？

translations.literal: What sort of rotten students are you lot, publicly violating the edict and testing the authorities?”
translations.idiomatic: What pack of bookworms dares defy an imperial edict and bait the law?”

sentences.zh: 」[七]亮曰：「亮含陰陽以生，戴干履坤。

translations.literal: ” [Note 7] Your servant Liang said: “Your servant was born between Heaven and Earth; how could he fear death when right calls?”
translations.idiomatic: ” Guo Liang answered, “Heaven and Earth gave me life to serve the right; your blade does not frighten me.”

sentences.zh: 義之所動，豈知性命，何為以死相懼？

translations.literal: Where righteousness moves him, how should he know life and bone—why use death to frighten him?
translations.idiomatic: When duty calls, what is my life to me? Threats of death do not move me.”

sentences.zh: 」亭長歎曰：「居非命之世，[八]天高不敢不局，地厚不敢不蹐。

translations.literal: ” The precinct chief sighed: “Living in an age not of [proper] fate, [note 8] though Heaven is high one dares not fail to crouch; though Earth is thick one dares not fail to tread lightly.”
translations.idiomatic: ” The warden sighed, “We live when Heaven itself is unsafe—though the sky is high we must walk bent double.”

sentences.zh: [九]耳目適宜視聽，口不可以妄言也。

translations.literal: [Note 9] ears and eyes fitly ought to see and hear; the mouth must not speak rashly.”
translations.idiomatic: “We may look and listen, but the mouth must stay shut.”

sentences.zh: 」太后聞而不誅。

translations.literal: ” When the empress dowager heard she did not punish him.
translations.idiomatic: The Dowager heard and spared him.

sentences.zh: 南陽人董班亦往哭固，而殉屍不肯去。

translations.literal: A man of Nanyang, Dong Ban, also went to weep for Gu and circled the corpse refusing to leave.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Ban of Nanyang likewise mourned and would not leave the body.

sentences.zh: [一０]太后憐之，乃聽得襚斂歸葬。

translations.literal: [Note 10] The empress dowager pitied them and thereupon permitted shroud and coffining, return and burial.
translations.idiomatic: Moved by pity, she allowed the rites of encoffining and burial at home.

sentences.zh: 二人由此顯名，三公並辟。

translations.literal: The two men by this became famous in name; the Three Excellencies together summoned them.
translations.idiomatic: Both men won fame and summons from the Three Excellencies.

sentences.zh: 班遂隱身，莫知所歸。

translations.literal: Ban thereupon hid his person; none knew where he had gone.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Ban vanished and was never traced.

sentences.zh: 注[一]續漢書曰，基，偃師長。

translations.literal: Comment [1] the Continuation of the Han Documents states that Ji was magistrate of Yanshi.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 1: the Later Han history says Li Ji governed Yanshi county.

sentences.zh: 袁宏紀曰，基字憲公，茲字季公，並為長史，聞固策免，並□官亡歸巴漢。

translations.literal: Yuan Hong’s chronicle states that Ji’s courtesy name was Xiangong and Ci’s was Jigong; both were senior clerks; hearing Gu was dismissed by edict, together they [damaged] office and fled home to Ba and Han.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Hong records their styles and says both were chief clerks who abandoned office and fled to the southwest when Li Gu fell.

sentences.zh: 南鄭趙子賤為郡功曹，詔下郡殺固二子。

translations.literal: Zhao Zijian of Nan Zheng was merit evaluator for the commandery; an edict came down to the commandery to kill Gu’s two sons.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Zijian, merit clerk of Hanzhong, received orders to execute the two sons.

sentences.zh: 太守知其枉，遇之甚寬，二子托服藥夭，具棺器，欲因出逃。

translations.literal: The administrator knew their injustice and treated them very leniently; the two sons pretended to take medicine and die young; they prepared coffin tools, intending thereby to escape.
translations.idiomatic: The governor knew the case was wrongful and colluded in a sham death by poison so they might flee.

sentences.zh: 子賤畏法，□吏驗實，就殺之。

translations.literal: Zijian feared the law; [damaged] clerks to verify the truth and thereupon killed them.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Zijian, afraid of the statute, sent clerks to verify the “corpses” and murdered both youths.

sentences.zh: 注[二]爾雅曰：「四達謂之衢。

translations.literal: Comment [2] the Erya states: “A place where four ways meet is called a crossroads.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 2: the Erya defines a major intersection as a “crossroads.”

sentences.zh: 」郭璞注曰：「交通四出者也。」

translations.literal: ” Guo Pu’s commentary states: “It is where traffic goes out in four directions.”
translations.idiomatic: ” Guo Pu glosses it as where four roads meet.”

sentences.zh: 注[三]謝承書曰：「亮字恆直，朗陵人也。」

translations.literal: Comment [3] Xie Cheng’s book states: “Liang’s courtesy name is Hengzhi; he was a man of Langling.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 3: Xie Cheng identifies Guo Liang as Hengzhi of Langling.

sentences.zh: 注[四]成童，年十五也。

translations.literal: Comment [4]: “completed boy” means age fifteen.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 4: “completed boy” means fifteen sui.

sentences.zh: 禮記曰「十五成童，舞象」也。

translations.literal: The Record of Rites states: “At fifteen, completed boy, he dances the elephant.”
translations.idiomatic: The Liji says that at fifteen a boy performs the “elephant” dance.

sentences.zh: 注[五]章謂所上章也。

translations.literal: Comment [5]: zhang means the memorial that was submitted.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 5: zhang here means the written petition he carried.

sentences.zh: 蒼頡篇曰：「鉞，斧也。」

translations.literal: The Cangjie pian states: “Yue is an axe.”
translations.idiomatic: The Cangjie word list defines yue as a battle-axe.

sentences.zh: 注[六]洛陽北面西頭門，門外有萬壽亭。

translations.literal: Comment [6]: the western head of the north gate of Luoyang; outside the gate is the Longevity pavilion.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 6: the Xia Gate was the western end of Luoyang’s north wall, beside the Longevity kiosk.

sentences.zh: 注[七]腐生者，猶言腐儒也。

translations.literal: Comment [7]: “rotten students” is like saying “rotten Confucians.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 7: “rotten students” means pedantic scholars.

sentences.zh: 注[八]非命謂衰亂之時，人多不得其死也。

translations.literal: Comment [8]: “not of fate” means a time of decline and chaos when many do not obtain their proper deaths.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 8: an “unnatural age” is one when men die before their time.

sentences.zh: 注[九]局，曲也。

translations.literal: Comment [9]: ju means bent.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 9: ju means “crouched” or “hunched.”

sentences.zh: 蹐，累足也。

translations.literal: Ji means feet piled [cautiously].
translations.idiomatic: Ji means walking on tiptoe in fear.

sentences.zh: 言天高而有雷霆，地厚而有淪陷，上下皆可畏懼也。

translations.literal: It means though Heaven is high there is thunder and lightning; though Earth is thick there is collapse—above and below alike are to be feared.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven sends thunder from its height; Earth opens chasms in its depth—both inspire dread.

sentences.zh: 詩云「謂天蓋高，不敢不局，謂地蓋厚，不敢不蹐」也。

translations.literal: The Classic of Poetry states: “They say Heaven is high, yet I dare not fail to crouch; they say Earth is thick, yet I dare not fail to tread lightly.”
translations.idiomatic: The Odes say, “They call Heaven high, yet I dare not stand straight; they call Earth thick, yet I dare not plant a firm foot.”

sentences.zh: 注[一０]殉，巡也。

translations.literal: Comment [10]: xun means to patrol [here: to keep vigil].
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 10: xun here means keeping vigil beside the bier.

sentences.zh: 楚國先賢傳曰：「班字季，宛人也。

translations.literal: The Former Worthies of Chu states: “Ban’s courtesy name is Ji; he was a man of Wan.”
translations.idiomatic: The Chu worthy Dong Ban, style Ji, came from Wan.

sentences.zh: 少游太學，宗事李固，才高行美，不交非類。

translations.literal: In youth he roamed the Imperial Academy and took Li Gu as his master; his talent was high and conduct fine; he did not associate with unworthy sorts.
translations.idiomatic: He studied at the academy, chose Li Gu as his teacher, shunned bad company, and was known for talent and character.

sentences.zh: 嘗耦耕澤畔，惡衣蔬食。

translations.literal: He once plowed in pairs beside a marsh, wore coarse clothes and ate plain food.
translations.idiomatic: He had farmed beside a marsh in rough cloth and simple fare.

sentences.zh: 聞固死，乃星行奔赴，哭泣盡哀。

translations.literal: Hearing Gu had died, he then by starlight traveled and rushed to go, weeping to the limit of grief.
translations.idiomatic: When Li Gu died he rushed by night to Luoyang and wept his heart out.

sentences.zh: 司隸案狀奏聞，天子釋而不罪。

translations.literal: The Metropolitan Governor investigated the facts and memorialized; the Son of Heaven released and did not punish.
translations.idiomatic: The Metropolitan Governor reported him, but the emperor pardoned the offense.

sentences.zh: 班遂守屍積十日不去。

translations.literal: Ban thereupon guarded the corpse, piling up ten days without leaving.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Ban kept vigil ten days without stirring.

sentences.zh: 桓帝嘉其義烈，聽許送喪到漢中，赴葬畢而還也。」

translations.literal: Emperor Huan praised his righteous ardor and permitted him to escort the funeral to Hanzhong; when burial was finished he returned.”
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Huan honored his loyalty and let him accompany the hearse to Hanzhong before returning home.”

sentences.zh: 固所著章、表、奏、議、教令、對策、記、銘凡十一篇。

translations.literal: What Gu wrote—memorials, petitions, submissions, discussions, instructions, policy replies, records, and inscriptions—in all number eleven pieces.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu’s surviving papers—memorials, petitions, edict responses, and the like—fill eleven items.

sentences.zh: 弟子趙承等悲歎不已，乃共論固言多，以為德行一篇。

translations.literal: His disciples Zhao Cheng and others grieved and sighed without end; thereupon together they discussed Gu’s words as abundant and made one piece on conduct and virtue.
translations.idiomatic: His students, led by Zhao Cheng, compiled his moral teaching into a separate memoir.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]謝承書曰：「固所授弟子，穎川杜訪、汝南鄭遂、河內趙承等七十二人，相與哀歎悲憤，以為眼不復瞻固形容，耳不復聞固嘉訓，乃共論集德行一篇。」

translations.literal: [Note 1] Comment [1] Xie Cheng’s book states: “Gu’s received disciples—Du Fang of Yingchuan, Zheng Sui of Runan, Zhao Cheng of Henei, and seventy-two others—together mourned in grief and anger, thinking their eyes would not again behold Gu’s form nor their ears hear Gu’s fine instruction; thereupon together they discussed and collected one piece on conduct and virtue.”
translations.idiomatic: Xie Cheng lists seventy-two disciples who collated Li Gu’s ethical teachings after his death.

sentences.zh: 燮字德公。

translations.literal: Xie’s courtesy name is Degong.
translations.idiomatic: Li Xie’s courtesy name was Degong.

sentences.zh: 初，固既策罷，知不免禍，乃遣三子歸鄉里。

translations.literal: At the beginning, when Gu had already been dismissed by edict, knowing he could not avoid disaster, he thereupon sent his three sons back to their home village.
translations.idiomatic: When Li Gu knew dismissal meant death, he sent his three sons home to Hanzhong.

sentences.zh: 時燮年十三，姊文姬為同郡趙伯英妻，賢而有智，見二兄歸，具知事本，默然獨悲曰：「李氏滅矣！

translations.literal: At that time Xie’s age was thirteen; his elder sister Wenji was the wife of Zhao Boying of the same commandery—worthy and possessed of wisdom; seeing the two elder brothers return, she fully knew the affair’s root and, grieving silently alone, said: “The Li clan is destroyed!”
translations.idiomatic: Li Xie was only thirteen. His elder sister Wenji, married to Zhao Boying in their home commandery and a woman of rare good sense, saw her brothers ride in and knew the worst; alone she whispered, “The Li family is finished.”

sentences.zh: 自太公已來，積德累仁，何以遇此？

translations.literal: From the Grand Duke downward they have piled up virtue and heaped up kindness—how could they meet this?
translations.idiomatic: “Since our grandfather’s day we have piled virtue—how have we earned this?”

sentences.zh: 」[一]密與二兄謀豫藏匿燮，託言還京師，人咸信之。

translations.literal: ” [Note 1] Secretly she plotted with the two elder brothers beforehand to hide Xie, falsely saying he had returned to the capital; people all believed it.
translations.idiomatic: ” She hid Li Xie with her brothers and spread word that he had gone back to Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 有頃難作，下郡收固三子。

translations.literal: Before long the disaster occurred; an edict came down to the commandery to arrest Gu’s three sons.
translations.idiomatic: Soon the warrant came for all three sons.

sentences.zh: 二兄受害，文姬乃告父門生王成曰：「君執義先公，有古人之節。

translations.literal: When the two elder brothers suffered harm, Wenji thereupon told her father’s disciple Wang Cheng: “You have held to righteousness for the former duke; you have the demeanor of the ancients.”
translations.idiomatic: After the elder brothers died she told Wang Cheng, “You kept faith with my father like a man of old.”

sentences.zh: 今委君以六尺之孤，[二]李氏存滅，其在君矣。

translations.literal: “Now I entrust you with the six-foot orphan; [note 2] whether the Li house survives or perishes lies with you.”
translations.idiomatic: “I entrust you with this boy—the Li line lives or dies in your hands.”

sentences.zh: 」成感其義，乃將燮乘江東下，入徐州界內，令變名姓為酒家傭，[三]而成賣卜於巿。

translations.literal: ” Cheng was moved by her righteousness; thereupon he led Xie east down the Yangzi, entered Xu Province’s territory, ordered him to change name and surname and be hired as a tavern laborer, [note 3] while Cheng sold divinations in the market.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Cheng took Li Xie down the Yangzi into Xu Province, hid him as a potboy in an inn, and himself peddled divinations in the street.

sentences.zh: 各為異人，陰相往來。

translations.literal: Each acted as a different person, secretly coming and going.
translations.idiomatic: They passed as strangers yet met in secret.

sentences.zh: 注[一]太公謂祖父合也。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: the Grand Duke means grandfather He.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 1: “Grand Duke” here means Li Gu’s grandfather Li He.

sentences.zh: 注[二]六尺謂年十五以下。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: the six feet means age fifteen and below.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 2: “six-foot orphan” means a boy under fifteen.

sentences.zh: 注[三]謝承書曰：「燮遠遁身於北海劇，托命滕咨家以得免。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Xie Cheng’s book states: “Xie fled far and hid his person in Ju in Beihai, entrusting his life to the house of Teng Zi and thereby obtaining escape.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 3: Xie Cheng says Li Xie hid in Ju county in Beihai with a man named Teng Zi—a variant of the tavern tale.

sentences.zh: 」與此不同。

translations.literal: ” This differs from this [account].
translations.idiomatic: ” That version differs from the story told here.

sentences.zh: 燮從受學，酒家異之，意非恆人，以女妻燮。

translations.literal: Xie followed him in receiving study; the tavern keeper marveled, thinking he was not an ordinary man, and gave his daughter in marriage to Xie.
translations.idiomatic: The innkeeper, seeing Li Xie’s brilliance, apprenticed him and married him to his daughter.

sentences.zh: 燮專精經學。

translations.literal: Xie devoted himself exclusively to classical learning.
translations.idiomatic: Li Xie buried himself in the Confucian canon.

sentences.zh: 十餘年閒，梁冀既誅而□眚屢見。

translations.literal: Within more than ten years Liang Ji was already executed and [damaged] prodigies repeatedly appeared.
translations.idiomatic: A decade passed: Liang Ji fell, and ill omens kept appearing at court.

sentences.zh: 明年，史官上言宜有赦令，又當存錄大臣冤死者子孫，於是大赦天下，並求固後嗣。

translations.literal: The next year the historiographers memorialized that it was fitting there be an amnesty edict, and also that the sons and grandsons of great ministers who died wronged should be sought out and recorded; thereupon there was a great amnesty for all under Heaven, and together they sought Gu’s surviving heir.
translations.idiomatic: The next year the court astronomers urged a general amnesty and a search for heirs of unjustly executed ministers; an edict pardoned the empire and specifically sought a descendant of Li Gu.

sentences.zh: 燮乃以本末告酒家，酒傢具車重厚遣之，皆不受，遂還鄉里，追服。

translations.literal: Xie thereupon told the tavern keeper the whole story from beginning to end; the tavern keeper prepared a cart and thickly sent them off with gifts—all he did not accept, and thereupon returned to his home village and afterward observed mourning.
translations.idiomatic: Li Xie confided the truth to his father-in-law, who offered money and a carriage; Li Xie refused everything and went home to mourn his kin.

sentences.zh: 姊弟相見，悲感傍人。

translations.literal: Sister and younger brother met one another; grief moved those beside them.
translations.idiomatic: Brother and sister embraced while onlookers wept.

sentences.zh: 既而戒燮曰：「先公正直，為漢忠臣，而遇朝廷傾亂，梁冀肆虐，令吾宗祀血食將絕。

translations.literal: Thereupon she admonished Xie, saying: “The former lord was straight and correct, a loyal minister to Han, yet met court turmoil; Liang Ji wantonly did violence, causing our ancestral sacrifices almost to cease.”
translations.idiomatic: His sister warned him: “Our father died a Han loyalist while the Liang clan ran riot and nearly wiped out our line.”

sentences.zh: 今弟幸而得濟，豈非天邪！

translations.literal: Now that you, younger brother, have luckily been able to cross through—is it not Heaven!”
translations.idiomatic: “That you survived is Heaven’s own mercy.”

sentences.zh: 宜杜絕觿人，勿妄往來，慎無一言加於梁氏。

translations.literal: It is fitting to shut off the throng of people, not rashly going back and forth; be careful not to add one word against the Liang house.
translations.idiomatic: Shun the crowd, speak no word against the Liangs.

sentences.zh: 加梁氏則連主上，禍重至矣。

translations.literal: To add words against the Liang house then connects to the ruler above; disaster would arrive doubly heavy.
translations.idiomatic: Any attack on the Liangs will touch the emperor himself and bring worse disaster.

sentences.zh: 唯引咎而已。

translations.literal: Only draw blame on yourself—that is all.
translations.idiomatic: Confine yourself to self-blame and silence.”

sentences.zh: 」燮謹從其誨。

translations.literal: ” Xie respectfully followed her instruction.
translations.idiomatic: Li Xie obeyed her to the letter.

sentences.zh: 後王成卒，燮以禮葬之，感傷舊恩，每四節為設上賓之位而祠焉。

translations.literal: Later when Wang Cheng died, Xie buried him with ritual, moved by gratitude for old kindness; every one of the four seasons he set out a chief guest’s seat and sacrificed to him.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Cheng died, Li Xie gave him a full funeral and each quarter set a place for him at the family sacrifice.

sentences.zh: 州郡禮命，四府並辟，皆無所就，後征拜議郎。

translations.literal: Provincial and commandery ritual summons, the four offices together summoned him—he went to none; later he was summoned and appointed Gentleman Consultant.
translations.idiomatic: He turned down every provincial and metropolitan summons until the court named him Gentleman Consultant.

sentences.zh: 及其在位，廉方自守，所交皆捨短取長，好成人之美。

translations.literal: When he reached office he was incorrupt and square in guarding himself; those he befriended all discarded the short and took the long, loving to accomplish others’ excellence.
translations.idiomatic: In office he stayed honest, chose friends for their virtues, and delighted in advancing others’ good names.

sentences.zh: 時穎川荀爽、賈彪，雖俱知名而不相能，燮並交二子，情無適莫，世稱其平正。

translations.literal: At the time Xun Shuang and Jia Biao of Yingchuan, although both were famous did not get along with each other; Xie befriended both sons, his feeling without partiality either way—the age praised his fairness.
translations.idiomatic: When rivals Xun Shuang and Jia Biao of Yingchuan would not speak, Li Xie befriended both without taking sides, and the world called him even-handed.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]論語曰：「君子之於天下也，無適也，無莫也，義之與比。」

translations.literal: [Note 1] Comment [1] the Analects states: “The noble man’s relation to all under Heaven is without insisting on this, without insisting on that—he compares himself to what is right.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 1: the Lun yu says the gentleman cleaves to right, not to factions.”

sentences.zh: 靈帝時拜安平相。

translations.literal: In Emperor Ling’s time he was appointed chancellor of Anping.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Ling he became chancellor of the kingdom of Anping.

sentences.zh: 先是安平王續為張角賊所略，國家贖王得還，朝廷議復其國。

translations.literal: Earlier the King of Anping, Xu, had been carried off by Zhang Jue’s bandits; the state ransomed the king and obtained his return; the court debated restoring his kingdom.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier rebels had kidnapped King Xu of Anping; after ransom the court debated restoring his fief.

sentences.zh: 燮上奏曰：「續在國無政，為妖賊所虜，守藩不稱，損辱聖朝，不宜復國。

translations.literal: Xie submitted a memorial stating: “Xu, while in his state, had no good government; he was taken by prodigious bandits; guarding his fief he did not measure up, shaming and insulting the sagely court—he ought not to have his kingdom restored.”
translations.idiomatic: Li Xie argued that the king’s capture proved his unfit rule and that restoring his state would shame the dynasty.

sentences.zh: 」時議者不同，而續竟歸藩。

translations.literal: ” At the time debaters differed, yet in the end Xu returned to his fief.
translations.idiomatic: The assembly split, but the king kept his domain anyway.

sentences.zh: 燮以謗毀宗室，輸作左校。

translations.literal: Xie for slandering the imperial house was sentenced to convict labor at the Left Construction camp.
translations.idiomatic: For impugning the Liu blood he was sentenced to hard labor in the Left Camp.

sentences.zh: 未滿歲，王果坐不道被誅，乃拜燮為議郎。

translations.literal: Before a full year had passed the king indeed sat guilty of immoral conduct and was executed; thereupon Xie was appointed Gentleman Consultant.
translations.idiomatic: Within the year King Xu was executed for debauchery, and Li Xie was vindicated with a consultant’s post.

sentences.zh: 京師語曰：「父不肯立帝，子不肯立王。」

translations.literal: A saying in the capital stated: “The father refused to establish an emperor; the son refused to establish a king.”
translations.idiomatic: Luoyang wits said, “The father would not make a bad emperor; the son would not save a bad king.”

sentences.zh: 擢遷河南尹。

translations.literal: He was promoted and transferred to Intendant of Henan.
translations.idiomatic: He rose to Intendant of Henan.

sentences.zh: 時既以貨賂為官，詔書復橫發錢三億，以實西園。

translations.literal: At the time offices had already been obtained by bribes; an edict again recklessly issued cash of three hundred million to fill the Western Garden.
translations.idiomatic: Office-selling was already rife when the throne ordered another three hundred million cash squeezed from the commanderies for the Western Garden treasury.

sentences.zh: [一]燮上書陳諫，辭義深切，帝乃止。

translations.literal: [Note 1] Xie submitted a memorial remonstrating; the wording’s meaning was deep and cutting; the emperor thereupon stopped.
translations.idiomatic: Li Xie’s passionate memorial stopped the levy.

sentences.zh: 先是穎川甄邵諂附梁冀，為鄴令。

translations.literal: Earlier Zhen Shao of Yingchuan had flatteringly attached himself to Liang Ji and was magistrate of Ye.
translations.idiomatic: Zhen Shao of Yingchuan had curried favor with Liang Ji as magistrate of Ye.

sentences.zh: 有同歲生得罪於冀，亡奔邵，邵偽納而陰以告冀，冀即捕殺之。

translations.literal: A fellow graduate who had offended Ji fled to Shao; Shao falsely accepted him while secretly informing Ji; Ji immediately arrested and killed him.
translations.idiomatic: When a schoolmate sought refuge, Zhen Shao sheltered him in public and betrayed him in secret; Liang Ji had the fugitive killed.

sentences.zh: 邵當遷為郡守，會母亡，邵且埋屍於馬屋，先受封，然後發喪。

translations.literal: When Shao was about to be promoted to commandery guardian, it happened his mother died; Shao for the nonce buried the corpse in the stable, first received his enfeoffment, and afterward issued the funeral.
translations.idiomatic: Zhen Shao was due for promotion when his mother died; he hid her corpse in a horse stall, took his seal, then announced her death.

sentences.zh: 邵還至洛陽，燮行塗遇之，使卒投車於溝中，笞捶亂下，大署帛於其背曰「諂貴賣友，貪官埋母」。

translations.literal: When Shao returned to Luoyang, Xie on the road met him, ordered soldiers to throw the carriage into a ditch, flogged him with random blows, and in large characters on silk on his back wrote “Flattered the mighty and sold his friend; greedy for office and buried his mother.”
translations.idiomatic: Li Xie ambushed him, overturned his cart, flogged him, and pinned a placard on his back: “Toadies to power, sells his friends, buries his mother for a title.”

sentences.zh: 乃具表其狀。

translations.literal: Thereupon he fully memorialized the circumstances.
translations.idiomatic: He laid the whole case before the throne.

sentences.zh: 邵遂廢錮終身。

translations.literal: Shao thereupon was cast off and barred for life.
translations.idiomatic: Zhen Shao was disgraced and banned from office forever.

sentences.zh: 燮在職二年卒，時人感其世忠正，鹹傷惜焉。

translations.literal: Xie died in office after two years; people of the time were moved by his family’s generations of loyal rectitude and all grieved and pitied him.
translations.idiomatic: Li Xie died in office two years later; the world mourned a house that had given the Han two generations of integrity.

sentences.zh: 注[一]事見宦者傳。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: the affair is seen in the treatise on eunuchs.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 1: see the chapter on palace eunuchs for Liang Ji’s fall and the amnesty.

sentences.zh: 杜喬字叔榮，河內林慮人也。

translations.literal: Du Qiao, courtesy name Shurong, was a man of Linlu in Henei.
translations.idiomatic: Du Qiao, style Shurong, came from Linlu in Henei commandery.

sentences.zh: [一]少為諸生，舉孝廉，辟司徒楊震府。

translations.literal: [Note 1] In youth he was a student; he was recommended as filial and incorrupt and was summoned to Minister over the Masses Yang Zhen’s bureau.
translations.idiomatic: As a young scholar he took the “filial and incorrupt” nomination and entered the bureau of Yang Zhen, Minister over the Masses.

sentences.zh: 稍遷為南郡太守，轉東海相，入拜侍中。

translations.literal: By stages he was promoted to Administrator of Nan commandery, transferred to be chancellor of Donghai, and entered to be appointed Palace Attendant.
translations.idiomatic: He rose to governor of Nan commandery, chancellor of Donghai, and Palace Attendant at court.

sentences.zh: 注[一]續漢書曰：「累祖吏二千石。

translations.literal: Comment [1] the Continuation of the Han Documents states: “For generations his ancestors were officials of two thousand piculs.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 1: Xie Cheng notes several generations of two-thousand-picul forebears.

sentences.zh: 喬少好學，治韓詩、京氏易、歐陽尚書，以孝稱。

translations.literal: Qiao in youth loved learning; he studied the Han school Classic of Poetry, Jing’s Changes, and the Ouyang Documents, and was praised for filial conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Du Qiao mastered the Han poetry tradition, Jing Fang’s Zhou yi, and the Ouyang Shang shu, and was known for filial piety.

sentences.zh: 雖二千石子，常步擔求師。

translations.literal: Although he was a two-thousand-picul’s son, he often went on foot carrying a load to seek teachers.
translations.idiomatic: Though his father ranked at two thousand piculs, he walked the roads himself to find teachers.

sentences.zh: 」林慮，今相州縣也。

translations.literal: ” Linlu is today’s county in Xiangzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Linlu is the modern Lin county in the Xiangzhou region.

sentences.zh: 漢安元年，以喬守光祿大夫，使徇察兗州。

translations.literal: In the first year of Han’an, Qiao was appointed acting Grandee of the Imperial Household and sent to inspect Yan Province.
translations.idiomatic: In 142 CE he served as acting Grandee of the Imperial Household on an inspection tour of Yan Province.

sentences.zh: 表奏太山太守李固政為天下第一；

translations.literal: He memorialized that Administrator of Taishan Li Gu’s government was first under Heaven;
translations.idiomatic: His highest praise went to Li Gu’s administration in Taishan;

sentences.zh: 陳留太守梁讓、濟陰太守汜宮、濟北相崔瑗等臧罪千萬以上。

translations.literal: Administrator of Chenliu Liang Rang, Administrator of Jiyin Si Gong, and Chancellor of Jibei Cui Yuan and others had corrupt crimes above ten million cash.
translations.idiomatic: He impeached Liang Rang of Chenliu, Si Gong of Jiyin, and Cui Yuan of Jibei for embezzlement exceeding ten million.

sentences.zh: 讓即大將軍梁冀季父，宮、瑗皆冀所善。

translations.literal: Rang was the Grand General Liang Ji’s youngest uncle by birth; Gong and Yuan were both men Ji favored.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Rang was Liang Ji’s uncle; Si Gong and Cui Yuan were the general’s protégés.

sentences.zh: 還，拜太子太傅，遷大司農。

translations.literal: On return he was appointed Grand Tutor to the heir apparent and promoted to Grand Minister of Agriculture.
translations.idiomatic: After the tour he became Grand Tutor to the crown prince and then Grand Minister of Agriculture.

sentences.zh: 注[二]蒼頡篇：「紱，綬也。」

translations.literal: Comment [2] the Cangjie pian: “Fu is the ribbon.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 2: the word list glosses fu as the official ribbon.

sentences.zh: 注[三]易旅卦九四曰：「旅於處，得其資斧。

translations.literal: Comment [3] the Changes, Lu hexagram, nine in the fourth, states: “The traveler finds a lodging; he obtains his profit axe.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 3: the Zhou yi’s Lu line speaks of the traveler who finds lodging and the “profit axe” of authority.

sentences.zh: 」前書音義曰：「資，利也。」

translations.literal: ” The Former Han Documents sounds and meanings state: “Zi means profit.”
translations.idiomatic: ” An old commentary glosses zi as “gain” or “advantage.”

sentences.zh: 益州刺史種暠舉劾永昌太守劉君世以金蛇遺梁冀，事發覺，以蛇輸司農。

translations.literal: Inspector of Yi Province Chong Hao impeached Administrator of Yongchang Liu Junshi for presenting a golden snake to Liang Ji; when the affair was discovered, the snake was delivered to the Grand Minister of Agriculture.
translations.idiomatic: Chong Hao, inspector of Yi Province, charged Liu Junshi of Yongchang with bribing Liang Ji with a golden snake; the snake was impounded by the Minister of Agriculture.

sentences.zh: 冀從喬借觀之，喬不肯與，冀始為恨。

translations.literal: Ji borrowed it from Qiao to view; Qiao would not give it; Ji thereupon began to harbor resentment.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji asked to borrow the exhibit; Du Qiao refused, and the general began to hate him.

sentences.zh: 累遷大源臚。

translations.literal: By stages he was promoted to Grand Herald [text reads 大源臚, error for 大鴻臚].
translations.idiomatic: He was promoted to Grand Herald, chief of diplomatic receptions.

sentences.zh: 時冀小女死，令公卿會喪，喬獨不往，冀又銜之。

translations.literal: At the time Ji’s young daughter died; he ordered the excellencies and ministers to attend the mourning; Qiao alone did not go; Ji again bore a grudge.
translations.idiomatic: When Liang Ji’s daughter died he ordered the whole bureaucracy to attend the funeral; Du Qiao stayed away, deepening the feud.

sentences.zh: 遷光祿勳。

translations.literal: He was promoted to Minister of the Imperial Household.
translations.idiomatic: He became Minister of the Imperial Household.

sentences.zh: 建和元年，代胡廣為太尉。

translations.literal: In the first year of Jianhe he replaced Hu Guang as Grand Commandant.
translations.idiomatic: In 147 CE he succeeded Hu Guang as Grand Commandant.

sentences.zh: 桓帝將納梁冀妹，冀欲令以厚禮迎之，喬據執舊典，不聽。

translations.literal: Emperor Huan was about to take Liang Ji’s younger sister as empress; Ji wished to order that she be welcomed with thick ritual gifts; Qiao relied on grasping the old canons and would not listen.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Huan married Liang Ji’s sister, the general demanded an extravagant bride-price; Du Qiao held to ancient precedent and blocked him.

sentences.zh: [一]又冀屬喬舉汜宮為尚書，喬以宮臧罪明著，遂不肯用，因此日忤於冀。

translations.literal: [Note 1] Moreover Ji instructed Qiao to recommend Si Gong as Master of Writing; Qiao because Gong’s corrupt crimes were clearly manifest thereupon refused to employ him; by this day after day he contradicted Ji.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji also ordered him to nominate the disgraced Si Gong for the Secretariat; Du Qiao refused and defied him daily.

sentences.zh: 先是李固見廢，內外喪氣，腢臣側足而立，唯喬正色無所回橈。

translations.literal: Earlier when Li Gu had been cast aside, within and without lost spirit; the throng of ministers stood on tiptoe in fear—only Qiao with correct mien had nothing of bending or yielding.
translations.idiomatic: After Li Gu fell the court went in fear of its shadow—only Du Qiao still met Liang Ji’s eye without flinching.

sentences.zh: [二]由是海內歎息，朝野瞻望焉。

translations.literal: [Note 2] By this within the seas sighed; court and wilds looked up to him.
translations.idiomatic: The empire drew breath in admiration; every eye turned to him.

sentences.zh: 在位數月，以地震免。

translations.literal: After several months in office he was dismissed because of an earthquake.
translations.idiomatic: He held the Grand Commandant’s post only a few months before an earthquake edict removed him.

sentences.zh: 宦者唐衡、左悺等因共譖於帝曰：「陛下前當即位，喬與李固抗議言上不堪奉漢宗祀。

translations.literal: The eunuchs Tang Heng, Zuo Guan, and others thereupon together slandered him to the emperor, saying: “Before, when Your Majesty was about to ascend the throne, Qiao together with Li Gu resisted and remonstrated, saying the sovereign above was not fit to continue Han’s ancestral sacrifices.”
translations.idiomatic: Eunuchs Tang Heng and Zuo Guan told the emperor that Du Qiao and Li Gu had opposed his enthronement as unfit to serve the Han altars.

sentences.zh: 」[三]帝亦怨之。

translations.literal: ” [Note 3] the emperor also resented him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor nursed a grudge.

sentences.zh: 及清河王蒜事起，梁冀遂諷有司劾喬及李固與劉鮪等交通，請逮案罪。

translations.literal: When the affair of the Prince of Qinghe, Suan, arose, Liang Ji thereupon instructed the authorities to impeach Qiao and Li Gu for communicating with Liu Wei and others, requesting arrest and investigation for crime.
translations.idiomatic: When the Liu Wei plot broke, Liang Ji had Du Qiao and Li Gu impeached as accomplices.

sentences.zh: 而梁太后素知喬忠，但策免而已。

translations.literal: Yet Empress Dowager Liang long had known Qiao’s loyalty; she only ordered dismissal by edict—that was all.
translations.idiomatic: The Dowager knew his loyalty and stopped at dismissal.

sentences.zh: [四]冀愈怒，使人脅喬曰：「早從宜，妻子可得全。

translations.literal: [Note 4] Ji grew all the more angry and sent men to threaten Qiao, saying: “If early you follow what is fitting, wife and children can be kept whole.”
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji sent killers with an ultimatum: kill yourself and your family lives.

sentences.zh: 」[五]喬不肯。

translations.literal: ” [Note 5] Qiao would not agree.
translations.idiomatic: Du Qiao refused.

sentences.zh: 明日冀遣騎至其門，不聞哭者，遂白執系之，死獄中。

translations.literal: The next day Ji sent riders to his gate; hearing no mourners, he thereupon reported and had him seized and bound; he died in prison.
translations.idiomatic: Next morning Liang Ji’s men listened at the door for funeral wails, heard none, burst in, and took Du Qiao to a cell where he died.

sentences.zh: 妻子歸故郡。

translations.literal: Wife and children returned to their home commandery.
translations.idiomatic: His family was sent home to Henei.

sentences.zh: 與李固俱暴屍於城北，家屬故人莫敢視者。

translations.literal: Together with Li Gu both were exposed as corpses north of the city; family members and old friends none dared look.
translations.idiomatic: His body was displayed beside Li Gu’s; no friend dared approach.

sentences.zh: 注[一]時有司奏曰：「春秋迎王后於紀，在塗則稱後。

translations.literal: Comment [1] the authorities memorialized: “The Spring and Autumn Annals in welcoming the queen from Ji, on the road then called her queen.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 1: officials cited the Annals rule for escorting a bride of state.

sentences.zh: 今大將軍冀女弟宜備禮章，時進征幣。

translations.literal: Now the Grand General Ji’s younger sister ought to have ritual insignia prepared, and in due course advance betrothal gifts.”
translations.idiomatic: They asked that Liang Ji’s sister receive the full queenly betrothal rites.”

sentences.zh: 」奏可。

translations.literal: ” The memorial was approved.
translations.idiomatic: The throne approved.

sentences.zh: 於是悉依孝惠帝納後故事，聘黃金二萬斤，納采鴈璧乘馬，一依舊典。

translations.literal: Thereupon everything followed the precedent of Emperor Xiao Hui’s taking an empress—two ten-thousand jin of gold for bride-price, geese, jade, and team horses, all according to the old canon.
translations.idiomatic: The wedding followed Emperor Hui’s precedent—two ten-thousand jin of gold and the full classical gifts.

sentences.zh: 注[二]回，邪也。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: hui means crooked.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 2: hui means “devious.”

sentences.zh: 橈，曲也。

translations.literal: Rao means bent.
translations.idiomatic: Rao means “bent” or “supple.”

sentences.zh: 注[三]抗，舉也。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: kang means to raise.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 3: kang means “to lift” or “present openly.”

sentences.zh: 注[四]續漢書曰：「喬諸生耿伯嘗與鮪同止，冀諷吏執鮪為喬門生。」

translations.literal: Comment [4] the Continuation of the Han Documents states: “Qiao’s student Geng Bo once lodged with Wei; Ji instructed clerks to seize Wei as Qiao’s disciple.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 4: Liang Ji framed a link between Du Qiao and Liu Wei through a student named Geng Bo.

sentences.zh: 注[五]從宜，令其自盡也。

translations.literal: Comment [5]: “follow what is fitting” means ordering him to kill himself.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 5: “take the appropriate course” was euphemism for suicide.

sentences.zh: 喬故掾陳留楊匡聞之，號泣星行到洛陽，乃著故赤幘，托為夏門亭吏，守衛屍喪，驅護蠅蟲，積十二日，都官從事執之以聞。

translations.literal: Qiao’s former clerk Yang Kuang of Chenliu heard it, wailed and by starlight traveled to Luoyang; then he put on an old red headcloth, pretended to be a Xia Gate precinct clerk, guarded the corpse mourning, drove off and protected against flies and worms, piling up twelve days, until the capital clerk for the department seized him and reported.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Kuang, Du Qiao’s former clerk, raced to Luoyang in mourning guise, posed as a gate guard, and kept the flies off both corpses for twelve days until the police arrested him.

sentences.zh: 梁太后義而不罪。

translations.literal: Empress Dowager Liang deemed him righteous and did not punish him.
translations.idiomatic: The Dowager called it loyalty and released him.

sentences.zh: 匡於是帶鈇鍎詣闕上書，並乞李、杜二公骸骨。

translations.literal: Kuang thereupon girded the fu-zhi, went to the palace gate, submitted a memorial, and begged together for the bones of the two dukes Li and Du.
translations.idiomatic: He then petitioned at the gate with axe in belt for the bones of Li Gu and Du Qiao.

sentences.zh: 太后許之。

translations.literal: The empress dowager granted it.
translations.idiomatic: She granted the request.

sentences.zh: 成禮殯殮，送喬喪還家，葬送行服，隱匿不仕。

translations.literal: Completing the rites of shroud and coffining, he sent Qiao’s funeral home, buried him, escorted in mourning dress, and hid away, not taking office.
translations.idiomatic: He encoffined Du Qiao, sent the hearse to Henei, and withdrew from public life.

sentences.zh: 匡初好學，常在外黃大澤教授門徒。

translations.literal: Kuang at first loved learning; he often in the great marsh of Waihuang taught disciples.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Kuang had taught students in the marshes of Waihuang.

sentences.zh: 補蘄長，[一]政有異績，遷平原令。

translations.literal: He was appointed magistrate of Qi, [note 1] his government having unusual merit, and was promoted to magistrate of Pingyuan.
translations.idiomatic: He served as magistrate of Qi with distinction, then governed Pingyuan county.

sentences.zh: 時國相徐曾，中常侍璜之兄也，匡恥與接事，托疾牧豕雲。

translations.literal: At the time the kingdom chancellor Xu Zeng was the elder brother of Regular Attendant Huang; Kuang was ashamed to join in handling affairs with him, pleaded illness, and herded pigs.
translations.idiomatic: When the kingdom chancellor proved to be the eunuch Xu Huang’s brother, Yang Kuang feigned illness and herded pigs rather than serve under him.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]蘄，今徐州縣也，音機。

translations.literal: [Note 2] Comment [1]: Qi is today’s county in Xu Province; it is read like ji.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Qi county lies in modern Xuzhou; the name is read ji.

sentences.zh: 注[二]袁山松書，匡一名章，字叔康也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Yuan Song’s book: Kuang’s other name is Zhang, courtesy Shukang.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 2: Yuan Song gives Yang Kuang’s alternate name Zhang and style Shukang.

sentences.zh: 論曰：夫稱仁人者，其道弘矣！

translations.literal: The essayist says: As for calling a man benevolent, his way is vast!
translations.idiomatic: The historian’s judgment: to call a man “humane” is to set a high standard.

sentences.zh: [一]立言踐行，[二]豈徒徇名安己而已哉，[三] 將以定去就之□，正天下之風，使生以理全，死與義合也。

translations.literal: [Note 1] Establishing words and practicing conduct—[note 2] how could it be merely pursuing name and securing self alone? [note 3] It is in order to settle the pattern of leaving or staying, correct the wind of all under Heaven, cause the living to be preserved by principle, and unite death with rightness.
translations.idiomatic: True words and deeds are not vanity; they set the moral weather of the age so that life rests on principle and death on duty.

sentences.zh: [四]夫專為義則傷生，[五]專為生則騫義，[六]專為物則害智，[七]專為己則損仁。

translations.literal: [Note 4] To devote oneself solely to rightness injures life; [note 5] to devote oneself solely to life betrays rightness; [note 6] to devote oneself solely to things harms wisdom; [note 7] to devote oneself solely to self injures benevolence.
translations.idiomatic: Single-minded zeal for duty can cost your life; single-minded love of life can forfeit honor; clinging to things dulls judgment; selfishness erodes humanity.

sentences.zh: 若義重於生，捨生可也；

translations.literal: If rightness weighs heavier than life, one may cast away life;
translations.idiomatic: When duty outweighs life, a man may die for it;

sentences.zh: 生重於義，全生可也。

translations.literal: if life weighs heavier than rightness, preserving life is permissible.
translations.idiomatic: when life outweighs duty, he may rightly save himself.

sentences.zh: [八]上以殘闇失君道，下以篤固盡臣節。

translations.literal: [Note 8] Above, because cruel darkness loses the lord’s way; below, because staunch loyalty exhausts the minister’s demeanor.
translations.idiomatic: Above, a vicious ruler forfeits the Way; below, a loyal minister spends his last measure of fidelity.

sentences.zh: 臣節盡而死之，則為殺身以成仁，去之不為求生以害仁也。

translations.literal: When ministerly demeanor is exhausted and he dies for it, then it is killing the body to complete benevolence; to leave is not to be seeking life and harming benevolence.
translations.idiomatic: To die when duty is done is “destroying the body to complete benevolence”; to withdraw without betraying humanity is not “clinging to life at benevolence’s expense.”

sentences.zh: [九]順桓之閒，國統三絕，太后稱制，賊臣虎視。

translations.literal: [Note 9] Between Shun and Huan the imperial line thrice was cut off; the empress dowager held the regency; villainous ministers glared like tigers.
translations.idiomatic: Between Emperors Shun and Huan three child emperors died, regents and eunuchs glared like tigers.

sentences.zh: 李固據位持重，以爭大義，確乎而不可奪。

translations.literal: Li Gu occupied his post, holding weight, to contend for the great rightness—firm and immovable.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu used his high office to fight for the right heir and would not be moved.

sentences.zh: [一０] 豈不知守節之觸禍，恥夫覆折之傷任也。

translations.literal: [Note 10] How could he not know that guarding integrity meets disaster—he was shamed by the thought that collapse would wound the charge entrusted to him.
translations.idiomatic: He knew integrity would invite ruin yet could not bear to fail the trust placed in him.

sentences.zh: [一一]觀其發正辭，及所遺梁冀書，雖機失謀乖，猶戀戀而不能已。

translations.literal: [Note 11] Observing how he issued straight words and the letter he left Liang Ji—although his stratagem failed and his plan went awry, still he clung and could not stop.
translations.idiomatic: Read his straight speech and his final letter to Liang Ji: his tactics failed, yet his heart could not let go.

sentences.zh: 至矣哉，社稷之心乎！

translations.literal: Supreme! The mind toward the altars of soil and grain!
translations.idiomatic: What depth of loyalty to the altars of state!

sentences.zh: 其顧視胡廣、趙戒，猶糞土也。

translations.literal: As for his looking back on Hu Guang and Zhao Jie, it was like dung and soil.
translations.idiomatic: In Li Gu’s eyes Hu Guang and Zhao Jie were no better than filth.

sentences.zh: 注[一]弘，大也。

translations.literal: Comment [1]: hong means great.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 1: hong means “vast” or “great.”

sentences.zh: 言非一塗也。

translations.literal: It means it is not of one path only.
translations.idiomatic: The “way” of the humane man admits more than one course.

sentences.zh: 注[二]立其言，必踐而行之。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: establish your words, you must practice and carry them out.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 2: what he professes he must live out.

sentences.zh: 注[三]徇，求也。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: xun means seek.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 3: xun means “to seek” or “pursue.”

sentences.zh: 注[四]□，節也。

translations.literal: Comment [4]: [damaged graph] means demeanor / integrity.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 4: the damaged character glosses as “moral bearing” or “integrity.”

sentences.zh: 立身之道，唯孝與忠，全生死之義，須得其所。

translations.literal: The way of establishing the person lies only in filial piety and loyalty; completing the rightness of life and death must obtain its proper place.
translations.idiomatic: Self-cultivation rests on filial piety and loyalty; life and death must each be accorded its proper moral weight.

sentences.zh: 注[五]貴義則賤生也。

translations.literal: Comment [5]: honoring rightness then debases life.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 5: to exalt duty is necessarily to devalue mere survival.

sentences.zh: 注[六]騫，違也。

translations.literal: Comment [6]: qian means violate.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 6: qian means “to violate” or “turn away from.”

sentences.zh: 注[七]為物則役智，故為害。

translations.literal: Comment [7]: being for things then enslaves wisdom; therefore it does harm.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 7: letting material things rule you enslaves the mind and does harm.

sentences.zh: 注[八]孟子曰：「魚我所欲，熊掌我所欲也。

translations.literal: Comment [8] Mencius states: “Fish is something I desire; bear’s paw is something I desire.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 8: Mencius’ dilemma of fish versus bear’s paw.

sentences.zh: 二者不可得兼，捨魚而取熊掌者也。

translations.literal: If the two cannot be obtained together, I forsake fish and take bear’s paw.
translations.idiomatic: If both cannot be had, he gives up the fish and keeps the bear’s paw.

sentences.zh: 生亦我所欲也，義亦我所欲也。

translations.literal: Life too is something I desire; rightness too is something I desire.
translations.idiomatic: Life is dear to him; so is moral duty.

sentences.zh: 二者不可得兼，捨生而取義者也。」

translations.literal: If the two cannot be obtained together, I forsake life and take rightness.”
translations.idiomatic: If he cannot keep both, he chooses duty over life.”

sentences.zh: 注[九]論語：「無求生以害仁，有殺身以成仁。」

translations.literal: Comment [9] the Analects: “There is no seeking life and harming benevolence; there is killing the body to complete benevolence.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 9: the Lun yu says one may die to preserve humanity but must not live to destroy it.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]確，堅貌也。

translations.literal: Comment [10]: que means the appearance of firmness.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 10: que describes rock-like firmness.

sentences.zh: 易曰：「確乎其不可拔。

translations.literal: The Changes states: “Firm indeed—he cannot be uprooted.”
translations.idiomatic: The Zhou yi speaks of unshakable firmness.

sentences.zh: 」論語曰：「臨大節而不可奪。」

translations.literal: ” The Analects states: “When facing great integrity he cannot be deprived.”
translations.idiomatic: ” The Lun yu says the gentleman at the supreme crisis cannot be swayed from his course.”

sentences.zh: 注[一一]易曰：「鼎折足，覆公餗。

translations.literal: Comment [11] the Changes states: “The tripod’s legs break; the lord’s thick soup is overturned.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 11: the ding hexagram’s broken legs that spill the sacrificial stew.

sentences.zh: 」言不勝其任。

translations.literal: ” It means not bearing up under one’s charge.
translations.idiomatic: ” The image is of a minister who fails under responsibility.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：李、杜司職，朋心合力。

translations.literal: The encomium states: Li and Du held office with one mind and combined strength.
translations.idiomatic: The verse-eulogy: Li Gu and Du Qiao served in harness, hearts as one.

sentences.zh: [一]致主文、宣，抗情伊、稷。

translations.literal: [Note 1] They brought their ruler to the level of Wen and Xuan; they matched in spirit Yi Yin and Hou Ji.
translations.idiomatic: They would have raised the throne to the level of Emperors Wen and Xuan, rivaling Yi Yin and Hou Ji in devotion.

sentences.zh: [二]道亡時晦，終離罔極。

translations.literal: [Note 2] The way perished, the times grew dark; in the end they suffered boundless false accusation.
translations.idiomatic: The age turned cruel; they met slander without limit.

sentences.zh: [三]燮同趙孤，[四]世載弦直。

translations.literal: [Note 3] Xie shared the fate of the Zhao orphan; [note 4] generation after generation they bore the straight string [of integrity].
translations.idiomatic: Li Xie lived the tale of the Zhao orphan; the line kept sounding the note of unbending rectitude.

sentences.zh: [五] 注[一]朋猶同也。

translations.literal: [Note 5] Comment [1]: peng means the same as “together.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 5: peng means “in league” or “of one party.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]伊尹、後稷也。

translations.literal: Comment [2]: Yi Yin and Hou Ji.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 2: the ancient ministers Yi Yin and Hou Ji.

sentences.zh: 注[三]離，被也。

translations.literal: Comment [3]: li means suffer.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 3: li means “to suffer” or “undergo.”

sentences.zh: 毛詩曰：「讒人罔極。」
