translations.literal: The Mao Classic of Poetry states: “Slanderers have no limit.”
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Poetry says slanderers know no bound.

sentences.zh: 注[四]趙朔之子趙武。

translations.literal: Comment [4]: Zhao Shuo’s son Zhao Wu.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 4: the “Zhao orphan” is Zhao Wu, posthumous son of Zhao Shuo.

sentences.zh: 史記曰，晉景公三年，大夫屠岸賈殺趙朔，朔客程嬰、公孫杵臼匿朔遺腹子於中山。

translations.literal: The Records of the Grand Historian states: in the third year of Duke Jing of Jin, great officer Tu’an Gu killed Zhao Shuo; Shuo’s retainers Cheng Ying and Gongsun Chujiu hid Shuo’s posthumous son in Zhongshan.
translations.idiomatic: Sima Qian records how Tu’an Gu slew Zhao Shuo and how Cheng Ying and Gongsun Chujiu hid the infant Zhao Wu in Zhongshan.

sentences.zh: 居十五年，後景公與韓厥立趙孤，而攻滅屠岸賈也。

translations.literal: After fifteen years passed, later Duke Jing with Han Jue established the Zhao orphan and attacked and extinguished Tu’an Gu.
translations.idiomatic: Fifteen years later Duke Jing and Han Jue restored the Zhao heir and destroyed the Tu’an clan.

sentences.zh: 注[五]載，行也。

translations.literal: Comment [5]: zai means conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary, note 5: zai means “to carry on” or “transmit.”

sentences.zh: 校勘記

translations.literal: Collation notes.
translations.idiomatic: Editorial collation (textual notes).

sentences.zh: 二０七三頁三行合在*

translations.literal: Page 2073, line 3: “should be placed with” [asterisk; lacuna in apparatus].
translations.idiomatic: Collation: page 2073 line 3—cross-reference marker incomplete in the source.

translations.literal: (Several [instances].)
translations.idiomatic: Editorial mark “several” in the apparatus.

sentences.zh: **[方]*術傳據集解引錢大昕說改。

translations.literal: **[Fang]* treatise on techniques and arts—emended per Qian Daxin’s argument in the collected annotations.**
translations.idiomatic: Emended per Qian Daxin to point to the treatise on techniques and arts.

sentences.zh: 二０七六頁一二行斗為天喉舌藝文類聚四十八引續漢書，「斗」上有「北」字，太平御覽五引本書，亦有「北」字。

translations.literal: Page 2076, line 12: “The Dipper is Heaven’s throat and tongue”—the Yiwen leiju 48 quotation from the Continuation of the Han Documents has the graph “north” above “Dipper”; the Taiping yulan 5 quotation from this book also has “north.”
translations.idiomatic: Note: parallel texts add “north” before “Dipper” in the quotation about Heaven’s mouthpiece.

sentences.zh: 按：校補謂據下文皆止言鬥，則「北」字非本有。

translations.literal: Editorial: the Jiaobu argues that since the following text speaks only of the Dipper, the graph “north” was not original.
translations.idiomatic: The collator argues “north” is a later interpolation because the sequel mentions only the Dipper.

sentences.zh: 二０七七頁二行*

translations.literal: Page 2077, line 2 [asterisk].
translations.idiomatic: Page 2077 line 2—apparatus lacuna.

translations.literal: (Variant: you / “still.”)
translations.idiomatic: Variant gloss “still” or “likewise.”

sentences.zh: **[由]*此言之據殿本改。

translations.literal: **“From this speaking”—emended per the palace edition.**
translations.idiomatic: Reading “from this it follows” adopted from the Dian edition.

sentences.zh: 二０七七頁一一行斟元陳樞按：殿本「元」下有「氣」字。

translations.literal: Page 2077, line 11: “ladle primordial, array the pivot”—note: the Dian edition has the graph “qi” after “yuan.”
translations.idiomatic: The palace text inserts “qi” after “yuan” in the apocryphon.

sentences.zh: 二０七八頁一０行腢下繼望刊誤謂「繼」當作「系」。

translations.literal: Page 2078, line 10: after the graph 腢—Jiwang’s Collation Errors says “ji” should read “xi” (tie).
translations.idiomatic: Note on a miswritten graph: one editor wanted “xi” instead of “ji.”

sentences.zh: 今按：繼亦音系，訓縛，亦維繫之義，見集韻，劉說非。

translations.literal: Editorial: “ji” also reads like “xi,” glossed as “bind,” and carries the sense of “maintain a tie”; see the Jiyun; Liu’s proposal is wrong.
translations.idiomatic: The collator defends the received reading with phonetic and semantic parallels in Jiyun.

sentences.zh: 二０七八頁一三行智者見變思刑愚者鶯怪諱名按：集解引惠棟說，謂「刑」通鑒作「形」。

translations.literal: Page 2078, line 13: “the wise see change and think of punishment; fools are startled by prodigies and conceal names”—the Jijie cites Hui Dong: the Zizhi tongjian writes “xing” as “form.”
translations.idiomatic: Variant: Hu Sanxing notes the Zizhi tongjian reads “form” for “punishment.”

sentences.zh: 胡注，此二語蓋本之緯書。

translations.literal: Hu’s commentary: these two phrases ultimately derive from weft-text apocrypha.
translations.idiomatic: Hu Sanxing traces the couplet to prognosticatory literature.

sentences.zh: 二０七八頁一四行加近者月食既於端門之側按：殿本「加」作「如」，考證云「如」字本或作「加」。

translations.literal: Page 2078, line 14: “add / draw near—then a lunar eclipse at full at the side of the Duan Gate”—note: the Dian edition writes “add” as “like”; the Kaozheng notes “like” in some editions appears as “add.”
translations.idiomatic: Collation on “add” versus “like” before the eclipse at the Duan Gate.

sentences.zh: 二０七九頁五行為其與*[公]*盟也據刊誤補，與公羊傳合。

translations.literal: Page 2079, line 5: “because he with *[the duke]* swore an oath”—supplied per Collation Errors; agrees with the Gongyang Commentary.
translations.idiomatic: Emendation restores “the duke” to match the Gongyang text.

sentences.zh: 二０七九頁一三行可敬*

translations.literal: Page 2079, line 13: “worthy of reverence” [asterisk].
translations.idiomatic: Apparatus note on “reverence” at page 2079 line 13.

translations.literal: (Variant: majesty / awe.)
translations.idiomatic: Variant gloss “majesty.”

sentences.zh: **[畏]*也據殿本改。

translations.literal: **“means awe”—emended per the Dian edition.**
translations.idiomatic: Reading “awe” confirmed from the palace edition.

sentences.zh: 二０八０頁一五行臣聞氣之清者為神至安國者以積賢為道按：集解引沉欽韓說，謂以上語並見繁露，「神」彼作「精」。

translations.literal: Page 2080, lines 15ff.: “Your servant has heard that what is clear in qi becomes spirit” down to “those who pacify the state take accumulating the worthy as the way”—the Jijie cites Shen Qinhan: the above language also appears in the Luxuriant Dew of the Spring and Autumn Annals, where “spirit” there reads “essence.”
translations.idiomatic: Parallel in Chunqiu fanlu uses “essence” for “spirit” in Li Gu’s memorial.

sentences.zh: 校補引柳從辰說，謂袁紀「神」亦作「精」，「練神」作「積精」。

translations.literal: The Jiaobu cites Liu Congchen: the Yuan Hong chronicle likewise writes “essence” for “spirit,” and “refine the spirit” as “accumulate essence.”
translations.idiomatic: Liu Congchen notes Yuan Hong’s chronicle agrees with “essence.”

sentences.zh: 二０八一頁一行軾段干木按：「段」原誤「□」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 2081, line 1: “raised the carriage bar for Duan Ganmu”—note: the graph “Duan” was wrongly written as a lacuna; corrected outright.
translations.idiomatic: Duan Ganmu’s surname restored from a corrupted blank.

sentences.zh: 注同。

translations.literal: The commentary likewise.
translations.idiomatic: The same correction applies to the commentary.

sentences.zh: 二０八一頁一一行子方在此按：集解引沉欽韓說，謂「子方」今新序作「大宗子敖」。

translations.literal: Page 2081, line 11: “Zifang is here”—note: the Jijie cites Shen Qinhan: in the current Xin xu the line reads “Grand Scion Ao of the great clan.”
translations.idiomatic: Shen Qinhan notes the Xin xu parallel names “Grand Scion Ao” instead of “Zifang.”

sentences.zh: 二０八八頁二行小子燮按：「燮」原皆斗「瀠」，汲本、殿本同，惟集解本不鬥，今徑改正。

translations.literal: Page 2088, line 2: “the youngest son Xie”—note: the graph “Xie” was everywhere miswritten as “ying”; Ji edition and Dian edition agree; only the Jijie edition is correct—now corrected outright.
translations.idiomatic: The name Li Xie was misprinted as ying in most editions; corrected to Xie.

sentences.zh: 二０八八頁三行乃左提章鉞按：集解引沉欽韓說，謂案文「鉞」字衍。

translations.literal: Page 2088, line 3: “then in his left hand carried the memorial and yue”—note: the Jijie cites Shen Qinhan: judged by the wording, the graph “yue” is redundant.
translations.idiomatic: Shen Qinhan argues the word “yue” (axe) is a scribal excess.

sentences.zh: 二０八八頁八行太后聞而不誅按：校補引柳從辰說，謂御覽三八五引李固別傳，作「太后聞而誅之」。

translations.literal: Page 2088, line 8: “when the empress dowager heard she did not punish”—note: the Jiaobu cites Liu Congchen: the Taiping yulan 385 quotation from Li Gu’s separate biography reads “the empress dowager heard and executed him.”
translations.idiomatic: Liu Congchen notes an alternate reading: the Dowager executed Guo Liang instead of sparing him.

sentences.zh: 二０八九頁七行司隸案狀奏聞按：汲本、殿本「案」作「察」。

translations.literal: Page 2089, line 7: “Metropolitan Governor investigated the facts and memorialized”—note: Ji edition and Dian edition write “察” for “案.”
translations.idiomatic: Variant graphs: “investigate” written 察 vs. 案 in different editions.

sentences.zh: 二０九一頁三行靈帝時拜安平相按：集解引惠棟說，謂華陽國志「安平」作「東平」。

translations.literal: Page 2091, line 3: “In Emperor Ling’s time appointed chancellor of Anping”—note: the Jijie cites Hui Dong: the Huayang guozhi writes “Dongping” for “Anping.”
translations.idiomatic: Hui Dong notes Huayang guozhi reads Dongping instead of Anping for Li Xie’s post.

sentences.zh: 二０九一頁九行先受封然後發喪按：刊誤謂甄邵遷為郡守，不得言「受封」，或「封」上脫一「璽」字。

translations.literal: Page 2091, line 9: “first received enfeoffment, afterward issued the funeral”—note: Collation Errors says when Zhen Shao was promoted to commandery guardian one cannot say “received enfeoffment”; perhaps the graph “seal” is missing above “enfeoffment.”
translations.idiomatic: Editors suspect a missing “seal” before “appointment” in Zhen Shao’s promotion line.

sentences.zh: 先受璽封謂拜郡詔也。

translations.literal: “First received the jade seal and appointment” means the edict appointing him commandery guardian.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss explains “seal appointment” as the commission to governor.

sentences.zh: 二０九二頁二行累祖吏二千石按：校補謂「祖」亦「世」字諱改。

translations.literal: Page 2092, line 2: “for generations ancestors were officials of two thousand piculs”—note: the Jiaobu says “ancestors” is also a taboo substitution for “generations.”
translations.idiomatic: “Ancestors” may be a euphemism taboo-avoidance for “generations.”

sentences.zh: 二０九二頁五行濟陰太守汜宮按：殿本「汜」作「泛」。

translations.literal: Page 2092, line 5: “Administrator of Jiyin, Si Gong”—note: the Dian edition writes “Fan” for “Si.”
translations.idiomatic: Dian edition uses Fan instead of Si for the corrupt official’s name.

sentences.zh: 二０九二頁一０行故陳資斧而人靡畏李慈銘謂「資」治要作「質」，即鍎字。

translations.literal: Page 2092, line 10: “therefore displayed the zi axe yet people were not awed”—Li Ciming states “zi” in the Zhengyao reads “zhi,” which is the zhi character [execution block].
translations.idiomatic: Li Ciming argues “zi” should be “zhi,” the executioner’s block, in the Zhengyao parallel.

sentences.zh: 今按：注引旅卦以釋資斧，則章懷所見本亦作「資」也。

translations.literal: Editorial: the commentary cites the Lu hexagram to explain “zi axe,” so what Zhang Huai saw also read “zi.”
translations.idiomatic: The commentary’s quotation supports the reading “zi” as in received texts.

sentences.zh: 二０九三頁一二行聘黃金二萬斤按：汲本、殿本作「一萬斤」。

translations.literal: Page 2093, line 12: “betrothal gold two ten-thousand jin”—note: Ji edition and Dian edition read “one ten-thousand jin.”
translations.idiomatic: Bride-price variants: one vs. two ten-thousand jin of gold.

sentences.zh: 二０九四頁二行喬故掾陳留楊匡按：集解引汪文台說，謂類聚九十七引謝承書，「楊匡」作「楊章」。

translations.literal: Page 2094, line 2: “Qiao’s former clerk Yang Kuang of Chenliu”—note: the Jijie cites Wang Wentai: the Yiwen leiju 97 quotation from Xie Cheng’s book writes “Yang Zhang” for “Yang Kuang.”
translations.idiomatic: Wang Wentai notes Xie Cheng gives the name Yang Zhang instead of Yang Kuang.

sentences.zh: 二０九四頁四行葬送行服按：王先謙謂「葬送」疑誤倒。

translations.literal: Page 2094, line 4: “buried, escorted, in mourning dress”—note: Wang Xianqian suspects “burial and escort” are reversed by error.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Xianqian proposes transposing “burial” and “escort” in the phrase.
